logEntries.text: Nigeria, 1965. You are born to a family of modest means but solid foundations, the newest in a large family. The sun is already through the window.

Your grandparent keeps you while your parents work. The day has a different texture — slower, full of a kind of attention you don't get elsewhere. There are stories about before you existed. You don't understand all of them. You keep them anyway.
The rainy season: everything planned around it. The dry season: everything planned around when it will end.
Biafra declares independence. A blockade follows. Up to two million die of starvation. The photographs reach the world too late.
You take your first wobbly steps across the living room floor. Your parents beam… — You make it three steps before sitting down hard. Everyone cheers.
The settlement extends in a direction that was empty land three years ago.
The rains do not come. Then they do not come again. The fields produce little. The market prices rise. Children in the village look smaller than they should.
The standpipe is shared between seven households. The schedule for the water is informal but respected.

There is a child at the compound gate or the water point or the end of the path … — You show them. They do not take it. They look at it the way you wanted them to look at it. Something is agreed without words.
Your early years are warm. There is food, laughter, and a sense that the world is safe.
The cooking fire is outside. The smoke goes up. The food takes the time it takes.

The civil war in the east has been running for two years. The photographs coming out — children with swollen stomachs, field hospitals in churches — have reached international newspapers and produced a new kind of response: emergency aid flows from strangers in Europe and America. The secession will not succeed. The question being argued is what the blockade has made of Nigeria's claim to unity.
Your father leaves before it is light. A bag goes with him. Your mother tells yo… — You are told something. The answer does not answer the question. You stop asking, which is its own kind of answer.
You and a classmate become inseparable. You spend every recess together and they invite you to their birthday party.
The lane to your door is navigable in the dry season and a negotiation in the rainy one.

Lagos is building itself faster than it can be planned. Cranes on every skyline. Roads half-finished. Imported cars in gridlock that didn't exist two years ago. A relative gets a government contract and buys a new car. The phrase "petrodollar" is new but everyone is using it. Whether the money reaches you or not depends entirely on who you know.
The early years end. You begin to know where you are.
There is a week when dinner is smaller each day. No one explains this. You learn to read the refrigerator like weather.

The price of everything made with petroleum — fertiliser, transport, plastics — is rising, and there is no cushion. Countries that import oil are paying twice what they paid last year for it. Food prices follow. The government has nothing in reserve to absorb it. The word that keeps appearing in the newspapers is one that was not there before: stagflation. You learn it without knowing what it means because the thing it describes is already in your kitchen.

The war ended with Gowon's phrase: "No victor, no vanquished." You are old enough to understand that this is a political sentence, not a description. The Igbo civil servants who left their Lagos posts to return to Biafra discover their positions have been filled. Old colleagues nod in corridors. The war that ended officially has not ended everywhere.

OPEC announces an oil embargo. The price of oil quadruples in weeks. Petrol queues stretch around blocks. In some countries the government introduces odd-even rationing. The relationship between the world and the ground beneath it has changed.
You spend every recess with the same person. You have a shared language now — re… — They come. Your bedroom becomes a world you've built together.

logEntries.text: Nigeria, 1965. You are born to a family of modest means but solid foundations, the newest in a large family. The sun is already through the window.
The go-slow on the bridge: the danfo at a standstill, the hawkers moving between vehicles.
You looked out the window at a particular moment. What you saw was unremarkable. You looked at it anyway.
Biafra declares independence. A blockade follows. Up to two million die of starvation. The photographs reach the world too late.
The radio has been on since before dawn. People are in the streets — you can hea… — You are inside the thing as it happens. You carry that inside it feeling for the rest of your life.
You grow up surrounded by siblings. There is always someone to fight with and someone to protect you.
The rains do not come. Then they do not come again. The fields produce little. The market prices rise. Children in the village look smaller than they should.
The baby has arrived and the house has reorganised itself around it. The baby cr… — You fetch the blanket and hand it carefully to your mother. She says good and that is enough.
A childhood illness passes through your area. You survive, but it leaves its mark.
You have a favourite spot. You have not told anyone it is your favourite.
The dry season and the wet season are two different countries. The smells change. The light changes. What is possible changes. You are learning which season is which.
The heat is a fact of the body all day and the management of the heat is part of every plan.

Biafra has declared independence. The federal blockade is total — nothing goes in, including food. The photographs reaching the world show children with the distended stomachs of kwashiorkor protein deficiency. The word that is not being used is famine. The word that is also not being used is starvation. One to two million Biafran civilians die before the secession collapses in January 1970. The federal government's reconciliation policy is announced as "No victor, no vanquished."
Code-switching is not thinking about switching. It is switching without thinking.

Lagos is building itself faster than it can be planned. Cranes on every skyline. Roads half-finished. Imported cars in gridlock that didn't exist two years ago. A relative gets a government contract and buys a new car. The phrase "petrodollar" is new but everyone is using it. Whether the money reaches you or not depends entirely on who you know.
The early years end. You begin to know where you are.

logEntries.text: China, 1952. The city is enormous and still growing. The neighbourhood you are born into will shape everything that follows.
The bell at the monastery, or the bells on the temple in the town, at 4am. The sound moves through the sleep.

Your grandparent keeps you while your parents work. The day has a different texture — slower, full of a kind of attention you don't get elsewhere. There are stories about before you existed. You don't understand all of them. You keep them anyway.
You take your first wobbly steps across the living room floor. Your parents beam… — You make it three steps before sitting down hard. Everyone cheers.

Your parent leaves in the morning. You have not yet learned to hold their absence well. When they come back, you run. This is the rhythm of the day. It will be years before you understand that they are leaving for you.
Sleep comes easily. The night is not complicated yet.
Your world is bounded by the walk your legs can make in a day. Everything outside that is rumor and adult conversation.
The restaurant was chosen before the price was considered. This continues to be a privilege.
The land as employer: the negotiation is with weather and soil, which do not negotiate.
Music comes from the next room. An adult you know is singing along, not performing — the unguarded singing of someone who does not know they are being heard. You stay where you are.
You did the ordinary thing on the ordinary day and it added up to the year.
The early years end. You begin to know where you are.
Your father leaves before it is light. A bag goes with him. Your mother tells yo… — You are told something. The answer does not answer the question. You stop asking, which is its own kind of answer.

Your grandparent cannot watch anyone leave food on a plate. It is not a rule that is stated — it is something in their face when food is wasted, something that goes past disapproval into a place you don't have the vocabulary for yet. You ask about it once. They say: "You don't know what we went through." They say it quietly, to no one in particular. They never say more.
The shrine is not formal. It is a shelf, a photo, a cup of water changed on Tuesdays.
The government's revolutionary programs cause the largest famine in history. Tens of millions die. The silence around this is total.

Market day is the week's fulcrum. Everything leads toward it or away from it — the produce set aside, the debts to settle, the cloth to examine, the information that only moves face to face. Without it the village would be only itself. With it, it is connected to every other village that sends someone.
The harvest: the days when everything else stops because this cannot wait.
You win a drawing competition at school. Your entry is pinned on the wall for three weeks. You are given something small to mark it. You carry it home with both hands.
The loose tooth is the central fact of the week. You cannot stop touching it.

There is one television channel. Every night it shows the same things: the leader visiting a factory, sports victories, news that is confident and untroubled. The gap between what it says and what your family talks about at the table is something you learn to manage very young.
The ancestral tablet on the shelf, or the shelf where it would have been before the flat did not have room.

One teacher whose name you will still know at eighty. Not because they were the favourite — although perhaps they were — but because something in the way they spoke to you was the first time you understood that an adult could take you seriously.
The children who went to the city: you know which households have had a child go. The going is the village's long-term project.
The body is changing. The world is starting to require something from you.

You go to the city for the first time. The noise. The smell of exhaust and food and people in numbers you have never seen. The buildings are larger than you pictured. You lose your companion in a crowd for twenty minutes and the feeling of it — pure, vertiginous panic — is also a kind of education.
The exam: the question you prepared for and the question you did not. The ratio was what the ratio was.
Tryouts for the school sports team are being held. The coach tells you that you … — You make the team. The early mornings are brutal but you love it.
The library after school: no adults who know where you are. The books are not the point, or not entirely.

The Red Guards arrive at the school. The teachers are made to stand in front of the students. The books are burned in the courtyard — which ones, exactly, depends on who is in charge of the bonfire. You learn to say the right things in the right order. What you actually think is kept entirely separate and never written down.

Red Guards fill the streets. Teachers are paraded in dunce caps. The libraries are burned or locked. Your family hides books under floorboards. Anyone with education, glasses, or soft hands is suspect. The rules rewrite themselves every week.
The grandparent's death this year: the first time you understood that the family is not constant.
You stay out past the time you were supposed to be home. Not by much the first t… — The punishment is real. So is the relief of having it handled.
The criticism session happens in the school courtyard. A classmate whose father … — You add your voice to what everyone is saying. The calculation is correct. The calculation does not dissolve.
Everyone is doing something and whether you join or don't is the thing being wat… — You belong, temporarily. The price of belonging is what you did or didn't do and whether you can live with that.
The rains came later this year than last. The planting adjusted.

Independence was ten, fifteen years ago. The flags changed. The faces on the currency changed. The underlying structure — who owns the land, where the export revenue goes, which families hold the contracts — has been slower to change. The leaders who led the independence movement are older now, and some of them have become the thing they replaced. You are old enough to notice this.
You are eighteen. The life begins in earnest.

The directive has come: educated youth must go to the countryside for re-educati… — Years of hard labour and isolation. You learn what your hands can do. You learn what loneliness actually is. You survive.
Everything heals faster than it will. This is being spent without being counted.
You graduate from high school. GPA: 2.90. The world is waiting — what comes next… — You enroll in university. The next four years will shape your career.
You are eighteen. The scaffolding of childhood has been removed. The life ahead … — You fix the compass on work and ambition. Other things will have to wait, or happen in the margins.
University — year 1 of 4 (healthcare).
You went somewhere and came back and the somewhere and the coming back were both ordinary.

The price of everything made with petroleum — fertiliser, transport, plastics — is rising, and there is no cushion. Countries that import oil are paying twice what they paid last year for it. Food prices follow. The government has nothing in reserve to absorb it. The word that keeps appearing in the newspapers is one that was not there before: stagflation. You learn it without knowing what it means because the thing it describes is already in your kitchen.

OPEC announces an oil embargo. The price of oil quadruples in weeks. Petrol queues stretch around blocks. In some countries the government introduces odd-even rationing. The relationship between the world and the ground beneath it has changed.
What will you study at university?… — Demanding, long hours, significant reward.

You are in a new city for the first time. There is no way to reach your family except by letter, which takes a week, or a long-distance call from a phone box with coins. You write a letter. You are genuinely on your own in a way that people born later will find difficult to imagine.
University — year 2 of 4 (healthcare).
Tea is poured before conversation begins. The conversation can wait for the pouring.

Someone close to you is in the using. Not you — them. The watching is its own pa… — The first meeting surprises you. The other people are you. The support is not about fixing the person you love — it is about managing the life that surrounds the not-being-fixed. That reframe is useful.

A woman sits alone in the church between services. She has not come for the service. The building is available to her in this hour in a way it is not when it is full of purpose. She sits without praying, or with a prayer that has no outward form.
University — year 3 of 4 (healthcare).
The city — or wherever you have arrived — is still being mapped. You are learning which streets lead where.
🎓 You graduate from university with a degree in healthcare. Final GPA: 3.00.
The airport is a country that belongs to everywhere and nowhere. You have spent days of your life in it cumulatively.

The earthquake strikes at 3:42 in the morning, when the city is asleep. The city of one million is built on alluvial sediment that amplifies the shaking. Most of the buildings are unreinforced brick. They do not survive. 242,000 dead — the official figure. Other estimates are higher. The Chinese government initially refuses all foreign assistance, a political decision made in the middle of a humanitarian catastrophe. Rescue comes from within: soldiers arrive overland because roads are gone, and begin to dig.
The clinic is busy. You wait two hours. The doctor is straightforward. Treatment… — The medication takes the edge off but the anxiety is still there, rerouted rather than resolved.
Everything moves slowly. Letters take days. Research means a library. Job listin… — Without distraction, you read everything you can get your hands on.
A confrontation at a bar escalates faster than expected. Someone throws a punch.… — You swing.
Bruised ribs, split lip. An expensive lesson.

The interest rate is lower than you feared. The monthly payment is manageable. You sign at three places and walk out with what you needed. The loan officer says congratulations. The weight of it arrives later — not all at once, but as a low-grade awareness that some of your future income already has an owner.
You made a decision this year you will not fully understand the consequences of for five years.

A graduation photograph being taken outside the building: the graduate in their gown, the family assembling around them. The photographer keeps asking them to move together. They do not know how to stand together as a group — they know how to stand as a family in their house, which is not the same. The photograph will look right anyway.
The temple is the centre of the village or neighbourhood in a way that does not require explaining.

The Special Economic Zones open. Shenzhen is a fishing village in 1979 and a city of millions by 1990. Factories absorb hundreds of millions of rural workers. The speed of it is unlike anything in economic history.

The departure gate: everyone waiting is going somewhere specific, and the somewhere is specific to each of them in a way the gate cannot accommodate. All the particular arrivals — the house, the hospital, the person who does not know yet that you are coming — held inside the general fact of people waiting for a flight.
The meal was ordinary and the company was good, or the company was ordinary and the meal was good. One or the other.
You are thirty. The life you have been building has become recognizable as a life.

A woman at the market has balanced a tray on her head for so many years that she does it without thought — reaching for a purse, turning to call to a child, all while the tray holds still. The skill is total. You have not learned anything to this degree.
The meditation before bed: the body settling, the thoughts not pursued.
You are thirty. The life you have been building has become recognizable as a lif… — The focus narrows. What exists gets deeper rather than wider.

A day when the rain makes the decision for you. You stay inside. You do not have to account for the decision because the weather has made it. There is a particular freedom in having the choice removed. By afternoon you are still inside and the rain is still there, and this is, in its way, a complete day.
The red envelope at Lunar New Year. The amount inside is a calculation based on relationship and circumstance.

You find a congregation or a community that is not one tradition — a Unitarian church, an interfaith dialogue group, a progressive mosque where multiple practices are honored. The theology is less precise. The community is stranger. There is something specific about worshipping alongside people who pray differently, who are trying at the same question by a different road.

A person outside a courthouse, alone, standing very still. Not upset, or past upset. Whatever was decided inside is now a fact and the fact is new and they have not yet integrated it into their understanding of how things are. They are standing with the new fact before they carry it anywhere.
The 3am wake: full consciousness arriving before you want it. The mind presenting its agenda without being asked.
The returnee: someone who went to the city and came back. The reasons for coming back are not always freely given.

You woke at three in the morning with a thought that did not warrant waking at three in the morning. The thought knows no proportionality. You lay with it for an hour. It dissolved into sleep before it resolved.
The blood pressure was checked. The number is what it is. You are doing what the number requires.
A message arrives from someone you knew decades ago — a childhood friend, a coll… — The catch-up is strange and warm. You remember a version of yourself through them.

Twenty-five years. You recognise everyone and no one. The person who was certain of everything at eighteen is a dentist in a different city. You talk about traffic and schools and prices. The past is very close and very far, and the gap between who you were and who you became is invisible to everyone in the room except you.
Students fill the square. The tanks come in the night. The protests end. No one speaks of it publicly again.

The back corner of the yard goes to seed one spring. The following year you buy seeds from the market. It becomes a thing you do in the mornings, before the day claims you. The plants do not know or care what else is happening. This turns out to be why you do it.
The factory is hiring. Then another factory. The city is growing faster than the maps can track. A generation is moving from the countryside to the skyline.
The body that knows the season before the calendar does: the knee, the shoulder, what the rain will do.
The photograph from twenty years ago: the face is yours and not yours. The not-yours is the part that requires looking at.
Someone finds you — a childhood friend, from before everything, from the years w… — It is a different friendship than the one you had. It is also something that could not be built any other way.

An old friend from school reaches out. They found you through something — a mutu… — The first hour is performance. The second hour is something closer to real. You leave with their number and a faint grief for the decades that happened differently for each of you.
The recipe came from someone who is not here to explain what they meant by "a handful."

The ask comes in a roundabout way — they say they hate asking, that they would n… — They are grateful in a way that slightly changes how they look at you. You both know this money will probably not come back.
The mortgage: the number and the term and the number of years the number will be the defining constraint.

The government has signed a paper in Washington. The details arrive incrementall… — You make the adjustments the situation requires. The word for this, later, is resilience. At the time it is just Tuesday.

A man sitting in a parked car outside a building, not going in. His hands are on the wheel. He is not going anywhere. The car is parked and he has been sitting in it for some time. When he finally gets out he gets out the way a person gets out when they have decided that going in is what comes next.
At some point in your forties the back becomes something you negotiate with. The… — The structured approach works. The pain is present but at a level you can manage around.

Britain returns Hong Kong. One country, two systems, the officials say. Families who left before the handover watch on television from Vancouver and Sydney. Those who stayed watch from rooftops. The uncertainty is the main weather.

The worst floods in forty years. Two hundred million affected. Hundreds of thousands of homes are gone. The Three Gorges Dam, still under construction, is part of the reason and part of the proposed solution.
Jun Chen calls on a Tuesday evening and talks for two hours. The marriage is end… — You are available through months of it. The friendship deepens in the specific way crisis allows.
You have worn the same dark suit or the same dark dress to funerals for fifteen years. It is the correct garment. You do not own a better one and do not need to.
A day when the rain makes the decision for you. You stay inside. There is a particular freedom in having the choice removed.
A message arrives from someone you knew before you became whoever you are now. T… — Something returns that you hadn't known was lost.

You grow up surrounded by siblings. There is always someone to fight with and someone to protect you.
The thing you wanted is not the thing you were given. This is being explained to you.

Your parent leaves in the morning. You have not yet learned to hold their absence well. When they come back, you run. This is the rhythm of the day. It will be years before you understand that they are leaving for you.
You did the ordinary thing on the ordinary day and it added up to the year.

An animal you knew is gone. You were told this in words, but the words haven't matched the reality yet. You go to where it usually was. It isn't there. This is the first time you understand what gone means — not absent, but permanently absent. There is a difference.
The high-speed rail between cities: the speed of it is not yet normal. The normal is arriving.

Men come to the door in uniforms and your mother takes you out of the room before they finish speaking. From the hallway you can hear the register of voices but not the words. Your mother's hand is tight on your arm. When it is over, nobody explains. This becomes one of the things nobody explains.
The slogan is on the wall. The slogan has been on the wall for so long it is now the wall.
The specific sound of your home at night. The pipes. The floorboard. The thing the street does at a certain hour.
The early years end. You begin to know where you are.

There is a child at the compound gate or the water point or the end of the path … — You show them. They do not take it. They look at it the way you wanted them to look at it. Something is agreed without words.

A child watching a funeral from a distance — from a window, or from the edge of the crowd where the adults have not noticed them yet. The child is watching the adults cry. Adults do not usually cry in public. The child is filing this under: things adults do that they normally do not do, which means: things that are serious in a way that ordinary things are not.
The government's revolutionary programs cause the largest famine in history. Tens of millions die. The silence around this is total.
There's a kid at school who has decided you are a target. Every recess. Every lu… — Your voice shakes but you hold your ground. They back off. Some of the other kids start talking to you after that.

Electricity comes to the village. Workers string cables along poles that appeared over several weeks and which you watched without understanding. Then one evening the bulb in your house is switched on. It is not a remarkable bulb. The light is not particularly warm. But the dark outside the window is different now — there is a perimeter where there was not one before. Your mother covers her face with both hands and laughs.

An animal you have given a name to dies. On a farm this is not unusual — you were told not to name them, which is why you named it. Someone buries it without ceremony. You are present for the burial. You do not cry, which surprises you, because you thought you would.
The smell of something cooking is the first announcement that someone is home.
Tea is poured before conversation begins. The conversation can wait for the pouring.

logEntries.text: In United States in 1955, the maternity ward is clean, the forms are in triplicate, and your parents drive home on a road with lane markings.
Your early years are warm. There is food, laughter, and a sense that the world is safe.
You do not understand everything the adults say. You understand more than they assume.
The cleaner comes on a scheduled day. Their name is known. The arrangement is professional and also human.

Your parent leaves in the morning. You have not yet learned to hold their absence well. When they come back, you run. This is the rhythm of the day. It will be years before you understand that they are leaving for you.
The meal was ordinary and the company was good, or the company was ordinary and the meal was good. One or the other.
One of your parents is gone. The room where they slept is still there. Their thi… — You are told something. The answer does not answer the question. You stop asking, which is its own kind of answer.

An animal you knew is gone. You were told this in words, but the words haven't matched the reality yet. You go to where it usually was. It isn't there. This is the first time you understand what gone means — not absent, but permanently absent. There is a difference.
The drive-through is an option. The option is taken.
The spring light returns at a different angle. People who were inside are now outside.
The world is large and you are starting to understand just how large.
The early years end. You begin to know where you are.
Someone brings a television into the house. Or a neighbour gets one and the stre… — You are told to move back. You move back two inches. You watch everything.
The teacher tells you to get under your desk and cover your head. You know — everyone knows — this would not actually help. You do it anyway.
The option you did not choose is still an option. Most choices work out for you at no particular cost.

For thirteen days, the adults are frightened in a way they cannot hide. The news is terse. The drills at school become something different. Then it passes and no one explains what would have happened.
You want a pet more than you've wanted anything in your short life. You dream ab… — Your parents say no firmly and repeatedly until you drop it.
The subsidy: the form, the compliance, the policy that changed and the adjustment to the policy.

A child watching a funeral from a distance — from a window, or from the edge of the crowd where the adults have not noticed them yet. The child is watching the adults cry. Adults do not usually cry in public. The child is filing this under: things adults do that they normally do not do, which means: things that are serious in a way that ordinary things are not.
Running is still the default speed. Walking is what adults do.
Karen Carter is better at something you care about — school, sport, the approval… — You stop measuring against them. Something in you relaxes.

Your results come back. They are good, better than most. A teacher reads your name aloud and the room shifts slightly in your direction. You do not know yet whether this is a gift or a burden or both.
You did the ordinary thing on the ordinary day and it added up to the year.
You spend every recess with the same person. You have a shared language now — re… — They come. Your bedroom becomes a world you've built together.
The portion size is larger than necessary. This has become the expectation.
The body is changing. The world is starting to require something from you.
Something embarrassing happened this year. You think about it more than necessary.
You find yourself drawn to making something. The feeling is specific — not a gen… — The instrument becomes the place you go when language fails.

A teacher stays behind to speak with you. They say something specific — not general praise, not encouragement-as-formula, but something specific to you: a sentence you wrote, a question you asked, a way you approached a problem. You have been seen in the particular, not the general, and that turns out to be a different thing entirely.
There is a person. You are aware of them in a way that has no useful precedent i… — You say something. It doesn't come out the way you rehearsed. It doesn't need to.
The airport is a country that belongs to everywhere and nowhere. You have spent days of your life in it cumulatively.

Fifty thousand women march down Fifth Avenue in New York. You watch the news footage or read the coverage. Something is shifting in how the question is being asked — not whether women deserve equality but when and by what means. The answer to the first question is no longer in dispute in the room you grew up in. The second question is being answered in the streets.
The cookbook was opened. Something new was made. This happens occasionally.
The draft lottery is on television. Men your age are going. Some come back. Some come back changed. The country argues about it at dinner tables and in the streets. No one agrees on what it is for.
There is a story going around about you. It is mostly untrue. The part that isn'… — The denial draws more attention to the rumour than it had. Eventually it fades, which is what all rumours do.

You need information for a school project. You go to the library. You look in the card catalogue. You find three books and read through all of them to find the two paragraphs you need. The information is imprecise and hard-won. You are more careful with it than you would be if it were easy to find.
You feel things at a frequency the years ahead will not sustain. You do not know this yet.
You are old enough to learn to drive. The independence it offers is real.… — The licence opens up the world in practical ways.
The body does what you ask of it without much consultation. This will not always be true.

OPEC announces an oil embargo. The price of oil quadruples in weeks. Petrol queues stretch around blocks. In some countries the government introduces odd-even rationing. The relationship between the world and the ground beneath it has changed.
Arab oil producers embargo the West. Fuel lines stretch around blocks. The postwar prosperity consensus cracks.
You are eighteen. The life begins in earnest.
The feast day of the patron saint of the town is the year's social calendar compressed into one day.
You graduate from high school. GPA: 2.76. The world is waiting — what comes next… — You earn a partial scholarship and enroll in university.
The year passed in the way that years pass — faster than the days suggested it would.
You are eighteen. The scaffolding of childhood has been removed. The life ahead … — You fix the compass on work and ambition. Other things will have to wait, or happen in the margins.
University — year 1 of 4 (healthcare).
The rosary at the end of the day. The beads are known by touch. The words arrive without thought.
What will you study at university?… — Demanding, long hours, significant reward.
University — year 2 of 4 (healthcare).
The coffee is ordered with a specificity that would have seemed unusual twenty years ago.
A professor takes notice of your work and offers to mentor you for a thesis proj… — You grow academically. The relationship opens doors.

Someone returning from a deployment, or a war, or a place that changed them in ways not visible in their face. They are at the bus station or the airport. Someone is waiting for them. The reunion is happening. Both of them are performing the reunion correctly. Neither of them is entirely the same person who was here when the deployment started.
University — year 3 of 4 (healthcare).
You have watched three people from the village leave. You know what they have now and what they no longer have.
Someone you met once, briefly, at a reception three months ago, calls with a nam… — The meeting goes well. The door is open. What you do with it is up to you.
🎓 You graduate from university with a degree in healthcare. Final GPA: 2.95.
The money has been invisible for long enough that its presence is harder to see than its absence would be.

The first genuinely warm day after a long winter: everyone in the city decides simultaneously to go outside. People who have not seen each other all winter are sitting on steps and in parks. The decision was made without coordination. The city is executing it with one mind.

65 countries do not go to the Moscow Olympics. The USA boycott, in response to the Soviet invasion of Afghanistan, takes their athletes with it. The athletes who spent four years preparing for this have their preparation rendered irrelevant by a diplomatic decision that neither side believes will change anything. The Soviet Union invades Afghanistan regardless. The athletes train anyway, for the 1984 Games, which the Soviet bloc then boycotts in return. Both boycotts accomplish nothing except the cancellation of four years of human effort.
The invitations change shape around this age. What was "come to the party" becom… — The wanting is real. The waiting has its own specific texture.

A disease with no name at first, then a name without a treatment. The deaths are concentrated in gay men, intravenous drug users, hemophiliacs. The government is slow to respond. By the time effective treatment arrives, a generation of men has been hollowed out of certain cities. You knew someone. Then you knew several people. Then you stopped counting.

You bought a coat that is not the cheapest option available. The decision took longer than it should have. The coat is good. You wear it more than any coat before it. You understand now why people talk about coats the way they do.
You meet someone. This feels different from before — more serious, more frighten… — A real relationship begins.

At eight in the evening, one office in the office building across the way is still lit. A figure at a desk, visible in silhouette. You are leaving. They are still there. You do not know if they are getting ahead or being left behind. Both can look the same from outside.
The single tree that marks the year — the same tree in the same position, season after season.

logEntries.text: In United States in 1955, the maternity ward is clean, the forms are in triplicate, and your parents drive home on a road with lane markings.
Your early years are warm. There is food, laughter, and a sense that the world is safe.
The washing machine is still the subject of comment — at how much easier it has made the day.
The letter is written by hand. The handwriting reveals the mood of the writing.
You looked out the window at a particular moment. What you saw was unremarkable. You looked at it anyway.
The fever comes at night. Your temperature is taken three times. The doctor come… — The treatment is unfamiliar and slightly frightening. You recover. Whether it helped is a question nobody settles.

An animal you knew is gone. You were told this in words, but the words haven't matched the reality yet. You go to where it usually was. It isn't there. This is the first time you understand what gone means — not absent, but permanently absent. There is a difference.
Someone brings a television into the house. Or a neighbour gets one and the stre… — You are told to move back. You move back two inches. You watch everything.
Your world is bounded by the walk your legs can make in a day. Everything outside that is rumor and adult conversation.
You do not understand everything the adults say. You understand more than they assume.

A page that was marks becomes words. You are not sure exactly when it happens — one morning the letters stop being separate things and start being a sentence, and the sentence is about a dog in a field, and the dog is there as clearly as if it were in the room with you. You read it again to make sure.
The ingredients are available regardless of season now. Winter strawberries exist. You are still deciding how to feel about this.
The early years end. You begin to know where you are.
A day when the rain makes the decision for you. You stay inside. There is a particular freedom in having the choice removed.

For thirteen days, the adults are frightened in a way they cannot hide. The news is terse. The drills at school become something different. Then it passes and no one explains what would have happened.
The choice is between two things of similar quality. The price is not the deciding factor.
You wake up to voices from downstairs — not angry-loud, but a kind of controlled… — They stop. Your mother says "everything's fine." You know it isn't but the night gets quieter.

One teacher whose name you will still know at eighty. Not because they were the favourite — although perhaps they were — but because something in the way they spoke to you was the first time you understood that an adult could take you seriously.
There's a kid at school who has decided you're the problem. It starts with words… — The teacher speaks to them. It gets worse for two weeks, then quietly stops.

A child watching a funeral from a distance — from a window, or from the edge of the crowd where the adults have not noticed them yet. The child is watching the adults cry. Adults do not usually cry in public. The child is filing this under: things adults do that they normally do not do, which means: things that are serious in a way that ordinary things are not.
The meal was ordinary and the company was good, or the company was ordinary and the meal was good. One or the other.

At the table nearby, the food is divided with a precision that suggests the calculation has been made many times. The children get more. The parents take less without commenting on it. The children eat without noticing. The parents do not want them to notice.
Food prepared by someone else always tastes differently than food you prepared yourself.
The airport is a country that belongs to everywhere and nowhere. You have spent days of your life in it cumulatively.
The body is changing. The world is starting to require something from you.
You fall for someone at school. It is consuming and slightly embarrassing.… — They say yes. It lasts a summer, but the feeling stays with you.
Tryouts for the school sports team are being held. The coach tells you that you … — You make the team. The early mornings are brutal but you love it.

You start keeping a record of things — thoughts, what happened, the shape of a feeling you are trying to hold in place. You don't call it anything. You hide it, not because it contains anything dangerous, but because the private nature of it is part of what it is.

Fifty thousand women march down Fifth Avenue in New York. Similar marches happen in London and Paris the same week. The demands are concrete: equal pay, childcare, abortion rights. The energy in the streets is different from anything you have seen — not grief or anger exactly but a kind of collective recognition, the specific feeling of a thing finally being named.
You told one person. One person. The thing you told them — specific, private, no… — They apologize. The apology is real. The friendship ends anyway.

A summer that feels different from other summers. You are not sure yet why it feels different, or rather you are not sure which of the reasons is the real one. Later you will know that it was the last summer that felt like this kind of summer. At the time you cannot know this. You just notice that something is different.
The coffee is ordered with a specificity that would have seemed unusual twenty years ago.
The draft lottery is on television. Men your age are going. Some come back. Some come back changed. The country argues about it at dinner tables and in the streets. No one agrees on what it is for.
Someone your age dies. An accident, or an illness, or something else. You were not close to them but you knew them. The funeral is the first one you attend where the person in the coffin is your age.
The restaurant was chosen before the price was considered. This continues to be a privilege.

OPEC announces an oil embargo. The price of oil quadruples in weeks. Petrol queues stretch around blocks. In some countries the government introduces odd-even rationing. The relationship between the world and the ground beneath it has changed.
Arab oil producers embargo the West. Fuel lines stretch around blocks. The postwar prosperity consensus cracks.
You are eighteen. The life begins in earnest.
Cooking for one involves the repetition of the same three things. The things are fine.
You graduate from high school. GPA: 2.94. The world is waiting — what comes next… — You earn a partial scholarship and enroll in university.
The neighbour's farm that was sold. The neighbour before that. The size of the holdings growing around the ones that remain.
You are eighteen. The scaffolding of childhood has been removed. The life ahead … — You fix the compass on work and ambition. Other things will have to wait, or happen in the margins.

Someone arrives in the city for the first time. You can tell by the way they stand at the exit of the train station with their bag and look at the street and then look at the phone and then back at the street. The city does not slow for arrival. The city has been here before you and will be here after you and is not interested in your first day.
University — year 1 of 4 (healthcare).
The solo life has its textures — some of them preferred, some of them not preferred, most of them both.
What will you study at university?… — Demanding, long hours, significant reward.

You have developed a morning ritual. It is small and private and probably unremarkable to anyone who is not you: the order of things, the specific quality of the first hour. You did not decide to develop it. It assembled itself. You notice, this year, that you look forward to it.
University — year 2 of 4 (healthcare).
Someone older told you something useful this year. You did not recognise it as useful until later.

In the station, everyone is going somewhere and has reasons for it. The man with the too-heavy bag has reasons. The woman in the yellow coat has reasons. The child running ahead of the parent has only speed as a reason, which is also a kind of reason. You find your platform. Everyone else finds theirs. The station empties in all directions at once.
University — year 3 of 4 (healthcare).
The supermarket is the weekly ritual. The trolley follows the same route. The choices are stable.

The people coming off the night shift at the hour when you are beginning. You pass each other at the shift change hour, going in opposite directions. They are wearing the end of the night. You have not thought about what the night cost them because you have been asleep for it.
🎓 You graduate from university with a degree in healthcare. Final GPA: 3.08.
You slept well last night. The not-noting-it is itself a kind of note.
The freedom is exactly that: the ability to be somewhere else by Thursday if it seems right.

65 countries do not go to the Moscow Olympics. The USA boycott, in response to the Soviet invasion of Afghanistan, takes their athletes with it. The athletes who spent four years preparing for this have their preparation rendered irrelevant by a diplomatic decision that neither side believes will change anything. The Soviet Union invades Afghanistan regardless. The athletes train anyway, for the 1984 Games, which the Soviet bloc then boycotts in return. Both boycotts accomplish nothing except the cancellation of four years of human effort.

A craftsman at work on something — the hands moving without instruction, the face entirely elsewhere. The object being made is almost incidental. What you are watching is the state of being completely in the right place.
A small thing happened that you will not remember in ten years. At the time it occupied you for an afternoon.

A disease with no name at first, then a name without a treatment. The deaths are concentrated in gay men, intravenous drug users, hemophiliacs. The government is slow to respond. By the time effective treatment arrives, a generation of men has been hollowed out of certain cities. You knew someone. Then you knew several people. Then you stopped counting.
University is disorienting and exhilarating. You meet people from everywhere and… — You graduate changed, and with people who matter.

The departure gate: everyone waiting is going somewhere specific, and the somewhere is specific to each of them in a way the gate cannot accommodate. All the particular arrivals — the house, the hospital, the person who does not know yet that you are coming — held inside the general fact of people waiting for a flight.
The building has its own sounds at night — the pipes, the lift, the street below.
A confrontation at a bar escalates faster than expected. Someone throws a punch.… — You swing.
Bruised ribs, split lip. An expensive lesson.

In the consultation, the interpreter is holding two people at once — translating not just words but weight, register, the thing said and the thing meant behind the thing said. Both sides think the interpreter is a conduit. They are not a conduit. They are doing something more difficult than speaking twice.
The neighbour above walks with a specific weight at a specific hour. You know this without having met them.
The view from the window at the same angle for years. The view has changed slightly. The angle has not.
You are thirty. The life you have been building has become recognizable as a life.
You are thirty. The life you have been building has become recognizable as a lif… — The focus narrows. What exists gets deeper rather than wider.
Nothing about the day required naming. The day did not mind.
Your sibling calls with big news — a marriage, a child, a move abroad. The years… — The bond holds across the distance.

You remembered a dream into the afternoon, which is unusual. Most dreams leave by nine. This one stayed. You are not sure what it means and are not inclined to interpret it formally, but it occupied a specific corner of the day.
A message arrives from someone you knew before you became whoever you are now. T… — Something returns that you hadn't known was lost.

The wall comes down. The world you were told was fixed — the two sides, the permanent standoff — is not fixed. People are walking through the Brandenburg Gate. History, which felt like a geological fact, is clearly not. Whatever comes next, you are watching the before and after happen in a single night.
The mobile phone is the size of a small brick and calls cost significantly. You use it for genuine emergencies.
An illness or accident generates a medical bill larger than your annual income. The debt follows you for years.

You see them across a room at a wedding. Your first love. They look the same and… — The conversation is short and warm and slightly disorienting. You leave it where it is. That version of you was a different person. This one is better.

You know which aisle has what. You know which product is never in stock on a Monday. You know the checkout queue to avoid. This knowledge, accumulated without effort over years of the same loop, is the kind of intelligence that goes unacknowledged. The weekly shop takes twenty minutes less than it used to. You have absorbed the shop.
The bicycle is the fastest route through the morning streets.

Your child brings home a school reader and asks you to help. You have been manag… — The silence after is short. Your child reads to you instead. Something between you shifts into something more honest.
The phone on the nightstand. The phone checked before sleep. The intention not to check it.

A friendship ends. Not in a confrontation — there was no clean break, no single … — The conversation is uncomfortable and clarifying. Some of what you did makes more sense in retrospect. You do not become close again but the ending has a shape now.

Children are playing in the street below, or in the courtyard, or on the patch of ground outside. You can hear them from where you are. The game they are playing makes sense only to them. They are entirely inside it. Something about the sound of it, heard from above, from the middle of an adult day, is both ordinary and not ordinary at all.

The notice goes up on Friday. The plant is closing. Thirty years of the same shi… — The retraining programs are underfunded and the jobs they train for pay half of what the plant paid. You take what there is. The difference between what you earned and what you earn is the shape of the next decade.

In the second-hand shop: a wedding photograph still in its frame, for sale at a low price. Two people on the best day of a year you cannot date from the photograph. Somewhere the context exists for what happened between then and here. The photograph doesn't know.

The internet moves from universities to homes, then to pockets. What information means, what distance means, what a shop is, what a friend is — all of it is changing faster than anyone can name accurately. The rules are being made up as it goes.

You and someone who was once your closest friend have not spoken in eight months. You notice this the way you notice a sound stopping. There was no argument. There was no event. There was a long sequence of unreturned calls and missed occasions and the gradual understanding that both of you had allowed this to happen without either of you deciding to.
The text message arrives. Its abbreviations are now fluent to you.

The man in the queue ahead of you buys the same things every week — you know this because you are in this queue every week — except that this week there is one of everything where there used to be two. You do not know if someone has moved out or died or simply stopped wanting the thing. You do not ask.
The CD player has replaced the cassette. The skip protection is still imperfect.

You caught a smell this year — something ordinary, something in a shop or a street or another person's kitchen — and for a fraction of a second you were somewhere else entirely, somewhere from your childhood. The specificity of the olfactory memory still surprises you.
The blood pressure was checked. The number is what it is. You are doing what the number requires.
The account was checked without anxiety. This continues to be unusual on a global scale. You know this intermittently.

Your grandmother, or someone else's grandmother, on the telephone. She is speaking to someone far away. Her voice on the phone has a quality it does not have in the room — brighter, more deliberate — as if she is performing being fine for the benefit of the distance. When she hangs up she is quiet for a while.
Someone finds you — a childhood friend, from before everything, from the years w… — It is a different friendship than the one you had. It is also something that could not be built any other way.
You have thought, more than once this year, about your own end — not with fear, with arithmetic.

In the morning, two planes hit the towers. By afternoon, a third hits the Pentagon and a fourth goes down in a Pennsylvania field. The tallest buildings in New York are gone. You watch the footage repeat on television. What follows — the security lines, the databases, the wars — will outlast everyone alive today.

You find a congregation or a community that is not one tradition — a Unitarian church, an interfaith dialogue group, a progressive mosque where multiple practices are honored. The theology is less precise. The community is stranger. There is something specific about worshipping alongside people who pray differently, who are trying at the same question by a different road.
Donna Johnson has done well. Not spectacularly — no headlines — but the house, t… — The envy passes. What remains is the friendship.

The ask comes in a roundabout way — they say they hate asking, that they would n… — They are grateful in a way that slightly changes how they look at you. You both know this money will probably not come back.
The hours have a different relationship to urgency now. Some things that seemed important were not.

The levees break. New Orleans floods. Eighty percent of the city is underwater for weeks. The response reveals something about which lives the state considers expendable. It takes years to rebuild, and some things are not rebuilt.
You are fifty. What you carry into this half is mostly set.
Your surviving parent can no longer care for themselves. The responsibility fall… — Years of your life reshape around their needs.
The stairs are taken one at a time in the mornings. By midday the stairs are fine.
You are fifty. The life you have lived has become visible, like a landscape from… — Not resignation — something quieter. The life is the life. You can live in it now.

A person in a park turning their face to the sun with their eyes closed — the particular quality of someone for whom this is not routine pleasure but something else, something they are paying attention to in a way that suggests the sun cannot be taken for granted right now. You don't know their situation. Their face tells you something.
The decision about whether they should still be living alone is a conversation being circled.
The GP refers you to a specialist. There is a wait. When you get there, the diag… — The medication takes the edge off but the anxiety is still there, rerouted rather than resolved.

logEntries.text: India, 1975. The city is enormous and still growing. The neighbourhood you are born into will shape everything that follows.
Your early years are warm. There is food, laughter, and a sense that the world is safe.
The firewood: collected, carried, stacked. The carrying is not labour that appears in any account.
You grow up surrounded by siblings. There is always someone to fight with and someone to protect you.
The head wobble contains meanings that require years to fully decode.
There is one thing that belongs to you. A piece of bright cloth, or a tin car wi… — You find a place for it that only you know. It stays yours.
Your world is bounded by the walk your legs can make in a day. Everything outside that is rumor and adult conversation.
The reservation system: the official remedy, the unofficial resentment, the gap between what is guaranteed and what is given.
Someone brings a television into the house. Or a neighbour gets one and the stre… — You are told to move back. You move back two inches. You watch everything.
There is a week when dinner is smaller each day. No one explains this. You learn to read the refrigerator like weather.
Saturday morning is a different quality of morning. The quality is something close to freedom.
The early years end. You begin to know where you are.

The standpipe at the corner runs from five until seven in the morning. You learn to be there at five, jerry can balanced on your head before school, queue behind eight others who are also going to school or work or nowhere in particular. The water that comes out is the same water everyone drinks. In the dry months it runs for forty minutes, not two hours.
The world is large and you are starting to understand just how large.

The haat comes on Thursdays. By seven in the morning the road from the river is already crowded — fish sellers, vegetable hawkers, the cloth merchant who comes from town. Your grandmother knows every price by memory and never pays the first one asked. The market is also news: what's happening in the next district, who married, which crop failed.
The week had a middle and an end and the end arrived.
School smells the same regardless of what day it is. You have filed this smell without meaning to.

The Union Carbide pesticide plant in Bhopal, Madhya Pradesh, leaks methyl isocyanate gas on the night of 3 December. 3,000 dead in the immediate aftermath; estimates of total deaths range to 15,000. 500,000 people are exposed. The CEO of Union Carbide, Warren Anderson, is arrested in India, released, and never extradited. The company is eventually acquired; the site remains contaminated. The survivors' compensation, settled in 1989, averages $550 per person.
The auto-rickshaw driver has a route opinion that differs from your route opinion. The negotiation is brief.
The idea of caste dharma is presented as natural order — each person born into t… — The framework provides structure. You fit yourself into it. It will take years to see what the fitting costs.
The city has a formal face and an informal one. You grew up in the informal one … —
You are dressed before the decision has been fully made. The mirror is consulted once. You leave.
The body is changing. The world is starting to feel more dangerous. The old habits of vigilance intensify.
There's a kid at school who has decided you are a target. Every recess. Every lu… — Your voice shakes but you hold your ground. They back off. Some of the other kids start talking to you after that.
The water is lower in the river now. Everyone has noticed.

Safety was not a given in your early years. The world taught you it could be tak… — Rules, routines, certainty manufactured wherever it can be. The world is not safe but your corner of it can be made to feel like it.

Your body is changing and it is doing so without your permission. People notice before you are ready for them to notice. Some of them say things about it. You learn to carry yourself in a particular way, a new way, that you did not have to think about before.
Nothing catastrophic happened this year. You noticed this in November, or December — the year closing without emergency.
You fall for someone at school. It is consuming and slightly embarrassing.… — They say yes. It lasts a summer, but the feeling stays with you.
The grandparent's death this year: the first time you understood that the family is not constant.
The Navratri fast: nine days of particular food, particular prayer, the garba dancing in the evenings.
The licence raj is ending. Foreign goods appear in the shops. New companies are being formed. The generation entering the workforce now will live in a different economy from their parents.
Something disrupts the order you have been maintaining. The plan fails. The old … — You do not stop. The wound is there but you will not let it stop you. This becomes a pattern.
The specific sound of your home at night. The pipes. The floorboard. The thing the street does at a certain hour.
The question of who you are becomes urgent. The answers available to you are mos… — The friction disappears. Something quieter replaces it.
The body is at the peak it may never discuss. You are not thinking about the peak. You are using it.
You are eighteen. The structures of childhood fall away. You will need to build your own. The life begins in earnest.
The word exists. You found it on the internet, or in a book, or someone else use… — The journey is long. The vocabulary is yours, now.
The thing you have been watching for did not arrive this year. The year ends with it still theoretical.
You graduate from high school. GPA: 2.40. The world is waiting — what comes next… — A practical path. Two years to a certified trade.
You walk somewhere today that most people take a vehicle to. The walking is the point.

The internet moves from universities to homes, then to pockets. What information means, what distance means, what a shop is, what a friend is — all of it is changing faster than anyone can name accurately. The rules are being made up as it goes.
You are eighteen. The scaffolding of childhood has been removed. The life ahead … — You fix the compass on work and ambition. Other things will have to wait, or happen in the margins.

The fire temple: the flame that has been burning continuously for — they will tell you how long — a very long time. Someone is tending it at this hour, which is a quiet hour. The fire is the prayer, the fire is the practice, the fire is the point. You watch them tend it without quite understanding and without needing to.
Trade school — year 1 of 2 (electrician).
The language of the home is not the language of the school is not the language of the government. You carry three.
Which trade will you train in?… — In-demand work. Good pay.

The interest rate is lower than you feared. The monthly payment is manageable. You sign at three places and walk out with what you needed. The loan officer says congratulations. The weight of it arrives later — not all at once, but as a low-grade awareness that some of your future income already has an owner.
🔧 You complete your electrician certification.
The seasons are the years. The year is measured from planting to harvest to the dry to the rains.
Diwali: the lights in every window, the smell of sweets being prepared, the firecrackers at night.
The spice box is consulted in a particular order. The order has not changed.

Bangalore and Hyderabad become global software centers. Engineering graduates who would have emigrated now find work at home at salaries their parents couldn't have imagined. The call centers employ millions. A new middle class forms on the back of a time zone.
You meet someone. This feels different from before — more serious, more frighten… — A real relationship begins.

logEntries.text: Iran, 1970. The country is in motion — politically, economically, always. You arrive into difficult circumstances from the first day.

logEntries.text: In West Germany in 1955, the maternity ward is clean, the forms are in triplicate, and your parents drive home on a road with lane markings.

Someone takes photographs of you. You will see them later — formal poses, an expression you won't remember making. You will not remember the moment of being photographed. Only the photograph. This is the first evidence of yourself that you will have.
The telephone call is short because it costs money. The shortness is efficient and slightly brutal.
The recipe came from someone who is not here to explain what they meant by "a handful."
Your early years are warm. There is food, laughter, and a sense that the world is safe.
The baby has arrived and the house has reorganised itself around it. The baby cr… — You fetch the blanket and hand it carefully to your mother. She says good and that is enough.
The annual leave is taken. The destination was agreed months ago. The planning was part of the pleasure.
Sunday is quiet in a way enforced rather than accidental.
Someone brings a television into the house. Or a neighbour gets one and the stre… — You are told to move back. You move back two inches. You watch everything.

A page that was marks becomes words. You are not sure exactly when it happens — one morning the letters stop being separate things and start being a sentence, and the sentence is about a dog in a field, and the dog is there as clearly as if it were in the room with you. You read it again to make sure.
The telephone rings. It is for one specific person. Everyone else finds out later.
The early years end. You begin to know where you are.

Something lives in the dark at the end of the hallway. Not always — only when th… — They sit on the edge of your bed and look at the hallway with you. Nothing is there. The looking-together helps more than the nothing.

For thirteen days, the adults are frightened in a way they cannot hide. The news is terse. The drills at school become something different. Then it passes and no one explains what would have happened.

Your grandparent has a silence about one decade that they do not have about other decades. You notice it without understanding it. When you ask about those years, they say something general that covers the years without opening them. The general answer is technically true. The silence around it is more informative.
The account was checked without anxiety. This continues to be unusual on a global scale. You know this intermittently.
You find a book that grabs you and doesn't let go. You read it under the covers … — The library becomes your territory. You run out of books to borrow and start over.
In the evenings the family gathers around the radio the way your parents once gathered around something else. The radio speaks to everyone and to no one. You do not need to look at it. You listen.
The meal was ordinary and the company was good, or the company was ordinary and the meal was good. One or the other.

One in sixty East Germans is an informant for the Stasi. Your neighbor, your colleague, possibly your spouse. You learn not to say certain things in certain rooms. After reunification the files will be opened. People will discover who reported on them. Some will not want to know.

The factory is the town. Your father works there or your uncle does or the father of every child in your class. The whistle at six in the morning means the same thing it means at three in the afternoon and at eleven at night for the night shift. The whole town runs on a schedule imposed by the factory. When you walk past it after school you can feel the heat from thirty meters away. The smell becomes so ordinary that you stop noticing it and then, years later in a different city, you smell something similar and for a moment you are nine years old again.
You went somewhere and came back and the somewhere and the coming back were both ordinary.
You discover a natural talent for sport. A coach takes notice.… — Sport becomes your identity.
The confession booth is a specific appointment. The priest has a specific manner. You know his approach.
The body is changing. The world is starting to require something from you.
A day when the rain makes the decision for you. You stay inside. There is a particular freedom in having the choice removed.
A regional scout watches your performance. You could be selected for a youth pro… — The selection changes what you think is possible.
The school uniform, if there is one, has an unofficial version — the collar, the shoes, the length — that communicates without saying.
An adult in your community takes a genuine interest in your future.… — The connection reshapes your understanding of what is possible.

Fifty thousand women march down Fifth Avenue in New York. You watch the news footage or read the coverage. Something is shifting in how the question is being asked — not whether women deserve equality but when and by what means. The answer to the first question is no longer in dispute in the room you grew up in. The second question is being answered in the streets.
At a house party, someone offers you drugs. "Everyone's doing it," they say.… — You try it. The experience is disorienting. You hope it was a one-time thing.

You start keeping a record of things — thoughts, what happened, the shape of a feeling you are trying to hold in place. You don't call it anything. You hide it, not because it contains anything dangerous, but because the private nature of it is part of what it is.
The cassette tape has the advantage of being skippable. You have recorded a side that is entirely what you want.
You have a question about christian_catholic that you cannot ask at home. Not a … — You hold the question alongside the practice. Many people do. You are not sure if that's wisdom or deferral.
The cookbook was opened. Something new was made. This happens occasionally.
There is ice on the path. This is managed.

OPEC announces an oil embargo. The price of oil quadruples in weeks. Petrol queues stretch around blocks. In some countries the government introduces odd-even rationing. The relationship between the world and the ground beneath it has changed.
Arab oil producers embargo the West. Fuel lines stretch around blocks. The postwar prosperity consensus cracks.
You are eighteen. The life begins in earnest.
The school is holding a formal dance. Someone has left a note in your locker ask… — A night you won't forget. Awkward and wonderful in equal measure.
The neighbour above walks with a specific weight at a specific hour. You know this without having met them.
You graduate from high school. GPA: 3.00. The world is waiting — what comes next… — You earn a partial scholarship and enroll in university.
You are eighteen. The scaffolding of childhood has been removed. The life ahead … — You fix the compass on work and ambition. Other things will have to wait, or happen in the margins.
University — year 1 of 4 (healthcare).
Food prepared by someone else always tastes differently than food you prepared yourself.
What will you study at university?… — Demanding, long hours, significant reward.
University — year 2 of 4 (healthcare).
The religious leader you trusted is found to have done something — financial, se… — You find a smaller congregation. Or you practice at home. The faith survives the institution, barely.

The man at the counter counts his coins slowly. There are not quite enough. He counts them again. He puts one item back. Nobody in the line says anything. He takes his things and goes without looking at anyone.
University — year 3 of 4 (healthcare).
You made a decision this year you will not fully understand the consequences of for five years.
You meet someone. This feels different from before — more serious, more frighten… — A real relationship begins.
🎓 You graduate from university with a degree in healthcare. Final GPA: 3.18.
Sarah wants to meet your family. It is a statement of intention — that they see … — The meeting happens. All meetings of this kind are complicated. This one is also theirs.

logEntries.text: Cambodia, 1965. The country is in motion — politically, economically, always. You arrive into a household of stability and warmth.
A childhood illness passes through your area. You survive, but it leaves its mark.
The radio, if there is one, is the village's shared newspaper.
A day when the rain makes the decision for you. You stay inside. There is a particular freedom in having the choice removed.

Your parent leaves in the morning. You have not yet learned to hold their absence well. When they come back, you run. This is the rhythm of the day. It will be years before you understand that they are leaving for you.
The baby has arrived and the house has reorganised itself around it. The baby cr… — You fetch the blanket and hand it carefully to your mother. She says good and that is enough.
Your world is bounded by the walk your legs can make in a day. Everything outside that is rumor and adult conversation.
You eat what is available. What is available is adequate, mostly.

There is a week when dinner is smaller each evening. Nobody says why. The pot is the same pot. The portions come out differently. You learn to eat what is given without asking if there is more. This is not taught to you directly. You learn it from watching the adults at the table.
The rains arrive on schedule or nearly — the sky changes in the afternoon, the smell before the first drop, then all at once.
The early years end. You begin to know where you are.

A parent at the school gate after the first day of school — watching the door that closed behind their child, then not watching it, then watching it again. The child is fine. The parent will also be fine. Neither of them knows this yet and the not-knowing is visible.
The harvest: the days when everything else stops because this cannot wait.
There's a kid at school who has decided you are a target. Every recess. Every lu… — Your voice shakes but you hold your ground. They back off. Some of the other kids start talking to you after that.

Market day is a weekly event that requires most of the previous day to prepare for. The walk to the nearest town takes two hours. Your mother wraps things in cloth and balances them. In the market there is noise, argument, news from other villages, the smell of things you do not eat every day. Someone tells your mother about a road being built. Someone else says a man from your village found work in Phnom Penh, in Siem Reap, in Battambang. The market is where the world arrives.
The queue for water or kerosene or the clinic is the first appointment of the day.

The price of everything made with petroleum — fertiliser, transport, plastics — is rising, and there is no cushion. Countries that import oil are paying twice what they paid last year for it. Food prices follow. The government has nothing in reserve to absorb it. The word that keeps appearing in the newspapers is one that was not there before: stagflation. You learn it without knowing what it means because the thing it describes is already in your kitchen.

OPEC announces an oil embargo. The price of oil quadruples in weeks. Petrol queues stretch around blocks. In some countries the government introduces odd-even rationing. The relationship between the world and the ground beneath it has changed.
Your results are fine. Neither disappointing nor remarkable. You sit with this information for a while, trying to work out how you feel about being exactly where you were expected to be.
The compound is swept in the morning. The sweeping is the first act of the day.
