logEntries.text: Brazil, 1970. The city is enormous and still growing. The neighbourhood you are born into will shape everything that follows.
Hunger is a recurring companion. You understand scarcity before you understand much else.
You are responsible for more of the visible daily life than the accounting would suggest.

Your parent leaves in the morning. You have not yet learned to hold their absence well. When they come back, you run. This is the rhythm of the day. It will be years before you understand that they are leaving for you.
The baby has arrived and the house has reorganised itself around it. The baby cr… — You fetch the blanket and hand it carefully to your mother. She says good and that is enough.
Jogo bonito: even when there is no match, the language of football is the ambient language.
The meal was ordinary and the company was good, or the company was ordinary and the meal was good. One or the other.

The price of everything made with petroleum — fertiliser, transport, plastics — is rising, and there is no cushion. Countries that import oil are paying twice what they paid last year for it. Food prices follow. The government has nothing in reserve to absorb it. The word that keeps appearing in the newspapers is one that was not there before: stagflation. You learn it without knowing what it means because the thing it describes is already in your kitchen.

At night there are sounds from outside that the adults do not explain. A vehicle… — They sit on the edge of your bed and look at the hallway with you. Nothing is there. The looking-together helps more than the nothing.

There is a portrait of the general on every classroom wall. You are taught that he saved the country from chaos. You memorize this the way you memorize multiplication tables — not because you believe it, but because you will be tested.
The market is your domain — the price-checking, the weight, the recognition of freshness. The vendor knows you.
The early years end. You begin to know where you are.

There is a child at the compound gate or the water point or the end of the path … — You show them. They do not take it. They look at it the way you wanted them to look at it. Something is agreed without words.
You slept well last night. The not-noting-it is itself a kind of note.

At the table nearby, the food is divided with a precision that suggests the calculation has been made many times. The children get more. The parents take less without commenting on it. The children eat without noticing. The parents do not want them to notice.
The night is dark when the power cuts. Dark in the way that has no gradation.
Your mother sells from a stall. Your father drives a motorbike taxi. The income … —

There is an old woman in a window across the street who watches everything. She has been there as long as you can remember. The watching is her occupation now — the street is what she has instead of going places. You find you wave at her sometimes.
Someone taller than you is right about something you were wrong about. This is uncomfortable and educational.
A teacher notices your effort and stays after class to help you.… — You absorb more than the lesson.

You see an adult crying — not at a funeral, not in the context that makes it make sense to you. Just at a table, or on the step outside, in the middle of an ordinary day. Adults cry for reasons you do not yet have. The world is larger than the reasons you know the names of.
A day when the rain makes the decision for you. You stay inside. There is a particular freedom in having the choice removed.
The day begins before sunrise. By the time the rest of the household wakes, several things are already done.
The body is changing. The world is starting to require something from you.
Food prepared by someone else always tastes differently than food you prepared yourself.
You see clearly how things work here: who pays whom, who gets what, why the rule… — You file it away.

The library in the late afternoon: each person at a different table, in a different internal world, in a different conversation with something that is not the room. The room holds all of them without asking what they are looking for. This is the arrangement: you come in with your question and the room is indifferent to the question and this is precisely what you needed.
A scholarship application lands in front of you. It would pay for university.… — You get it. The letter arrives on a Tuesday.
Prices change mid-shopping. Supermarkets reprice shelves daily. Savings kept in cash lose half their value in a week. The economy teaches everyone a lesson about the unreality of numbers.
The offering left at the threshold: not superstition but acknowledgement that things exist beyond what can be measured.
Your body has become a subject of comment — from family, from boys, from other g… — A reframing that takes years to fully land. It helps.
The list in your head does not empty. It reconfigures. By the time one thing is done, three others have moved up.
Walking home. The calculations you run every time: which street, which time, how… —
The city is where the future is supposed to be. The question of whether to go is the question of this decade.
You are eighteen. The life begins in earnest.
The school-leaving certificate is the document that makes or doesn't make the fu… — The results, when they come, reflect what you put in. The door is open.
Hyperinflation. Prices change while you queue. You lose a significant portion of your savings to currency collapse.
You graduate from high school. GPA: 2.64. The world is waiting — what comes next… — You earn a partial scholarship and enroll in university.
You looked out the window at a particular moment. What you saw was unremarkable. You looked at it anyway.
You are eighteen. The scaffolding of childhood has been removed. The life ahead … — You fix the compass on work and ambition. Other things will have to wait, or happen in the margins.
University — year 1 of 4 (healthcare).
What will you study at university?… — Demanding, long hours, significant reward.
You are 22. You are studying. The future is still mostly theoretical. This is the beginning of something, though you cannot yet see its shape.
University — year 2 of 4 (healthcare).
The children who went to the city: you know which households have had a child go. The going is the village's long-term project.

October 2, 1992. Military police enter Carandiru prison in São Paulo after a riot. In thirty minutes, 111 prisoners are dead — shot, many execution-style. No guards die. The prison holds 7,000 people in a facility built for 3,500. It is the single deadliest act of state violence in Brazil since the dictatorship ended. The government calls it a restoration of order.
The notice is under your door when you come in. Section 21, or its equivalent — … — The search is harder than you expected. You have a month left and two realistic options, both worse than this.

You have developed a morning ritual. It is small and private and probably unremarkable to anyone who is not you: the order of things, the specific quality of the first hour. You did not decide to develop it. It assembled itself. You notice, this year, that you look forward to it.
University — year 3 of 4 (healthcare).

The queue is long and no one is complaining. Something about the acceptance in the queue — everyone here has calculated that what they are waiting for is worth the wait. They stand with the patience of people who have made a decision and are living inside it.
🎓 You graduate from university with a degree in healthcare. The first in your family to do so. Final GPA: 2.73.

The price tags have been changing daily. Monthly. The supermarkets have had people following the shelf-stackers to change the labels before the customers can get to the goods. Then, on July 1, 1994, the Real. The new currency. The inflation that has been as high as two thousand percent a year comes down. Prices stabilise. People can plan. The specific feeling of a litre of milk costing what it cost last week — this is not trivial. This is what economic stability means when you have not had it.

The new currency holds. Inflation — which had been 2,500% a year — collapses almost overnight. Prices stabilize. Workers can save for the first time in a generation. The middle class begins to form itself.
The water is fetched. The fetching is not counted as work because it happens before work begins.

The internet moves from universities to homes, then to pockets. What information means, what distance means, what a shop is, what a friend is — all of it is changing faster than anyone can name accurately. The rules are being made up as it goes.
You are the first in your family to finish a university degree. The graduation ceremony is strange — joy and strangeness mixed.
You stayed up too late and the morning is paying for it. The payment is manageable.
The clinic is busy. You wait two hours. The doctor is straightforward. Treatment… — The medication quiets the alarm system enough to live with. Some days are genuinely fine.
The children's illness falls to you. The school appointment falls to you. The in-law visit falls to you.
The airport is a country that belongs to everywhere and nowhere. You have spent days of your life in it cumulatively.
Something is happening politically in your country. You feel the pull to act.… — You stand for something. The cost is uncertain.

logEntries.text: South Africa, 1960. The city is enormous and still growing. The neighbourhood you are born into will shape everything that follows.
One of your parents drinks. You understand what drunk looks like before you understand much else. The smell is particular. The unpredictability is worse.
The week had a middle and an end and the end arrived.
Race determines where you live, where you work, what school you attend. The law enforces a hierarchy that crushes some and privileges others.

Your parent leaves in the morning. You have not yet learned to hold their absence well. When they come back, you run. This is the rhythm of the day. It will be years before you understand that they are leaving for you.
The packed lunch is the economics of the day: made at home, eaten at work, nothing spent on a meal.

The world is full of things to climb and fall from. You are learning both. A knee scraped on stone. The way height looks from somewhere you're not supposed to be. The way your body is more resilient than you expected, and sometimes less.
The compound or the courtyard is the center of everything. Life happens at close range — you know everyone's business because there is no room not to.
The conductor is calling the route from the window. The route is the route you take.
Hunger is a recurring companion. You understand scarcity before you understand much else.
You went somewhere and came back and the somewhere and the coming back were both ordinary.

The standpipe at the corner runs from five until seven in the morning. You learn to be there at five, jerry can balanced on your head before school, queue behind eight others who are also going to school or work or nowhere in particular. The water that comes out is the same water everyone drinks. In the dry months it runs for forty minutes, not two hours.
The hymn arrives without prompting. You are doing something else entirely when it appears.
The early years end. You begin to know where you are.
You discover the school library. The librarian lets you take out more books than the limit.
The work is physical and the body carries the record of the years. By evening the back has opinions.

Your parents told you early, before you could fully understand it. The story they tell is about choosing — they wanted you specifically, from all the children. You accept this story and also notice, later, that it is a story made for the child rather than the fact. The fact is more complicated and you are old enough now to sense the edges of it.
A day when the rain makes the decision for you. You stay inside. There is a particular freedom in having the choice removed.
An older child at school has made you their target. It happens daily now.… — You win enough respect to be left alone. Your knuckles ache.

A group you considered yourself part of does not include you in something. You find out later — through the carelessness of someone who assumed you already knew, or through the specific silence of people who share a thing you are not sharing. You say nothing about it to anyone. You reassess.
The boda-boda, okada, tuk-tuk — the driver knows which streets are faster today. You trust this.
The things that your colleagues assume as given that you did not come with. You learned them. The learning is invisible now.

Thokoza has a logic that outsiders miss. Who owns which block of pavement. Where the water pressure is best in the morning. Which family has the generator when the power cuts. You know these things the way you know your own face. This knowledge is not nothing.
The bill arrived. The bill is paid. The paying of it leaves something specific in the account.
The body is changing. The world is starting to require something from you.
You wake up to voices from downstairs — not angry-loud, but a kind of controlled… — They stop. Your mother says "everything's fine." You know it isn't but the night gets quieter.
The tithe is the tithe. The first tenth before anything else.

The price of everything made with petroleum — fertiliser, transport, plastics — is rising, and there is no cushion. Countries that import oil are paying twice what they paid last year for it. Food prices follow. The government has nothing in reserve to absorb it. The word that keeps appearing in the newspapers is one that was not there before: stagflation. You learn it without knowing what it means because the thing it describes is already in your kitchen.

OPEC announces an oil embargo. The price of oil quadruples in weeks. Petrol queues stretch around blocks. In some countries the government introduces odd-even rationing. The relationship between the world and the ground beneath it has changed.
Confirmation. You stand before the congregation and affirm that the faith is you… — You feel the weight of what you are saying. You say it anyway. Something settles.

Independence was ten, fifteen years ago. The flags changed. The faces on the currency changed. The underlying structure — who owns the land, where the export revenue goes, which families hold the contracts — has been slower to change. The leaders who led the independence movement are older now, and some of them have become the thing they replaced. You are old enough to notice this.

West Indies win the World Cup while South Africa is banned from international cricket because of apartheid. The irony is not lost on everyone — a team of Black West Indian cricketers wins the world championship while the country that enforced racial hierarchy in sport cannot participate. South African cricket does not allow Black South Africans to represent the country at any level.
Confirmation class meets on Wednesday evenings. You're supposed to affirm that y… — You stand at the altar. Everyone claps. Your grandmother cries. You feel something, though you're not sure what.

The pass book must be carried at all times. Failure to produce it means arrest. The book determines where you may live, where you may work, which train you may board. It is a document that describes your permitted life in a country you were born in.

The pass book must be carried at all times. Without it you cannot legally be in the city after dark, cannot take certain jobs, cannot live in certain areas. The book is checked at roadblocks, at building entrances, by police who can detain you on its absence. It is a document that defines what you are allowed to be.
The school is holding a formal dance. Someone has left a note in your locker ask… — A night you won't forget. Awkward and wonderful in equal measure.

You are out at night, later than you have been out before, without adults who are responsible for you. The streets feel different at this hour. Your friends are different too — louder, more deliberate, performing something. You are not sure if you are performing too. You think probably yes.
The shift pattern divides the week differently from everywhere else. The week is underground and above ground, in rotation.
At a revival meeting, the pastor calls people forward. The music is overwhelming… — You are born again. The congregation embraces you. For weeks the world looks different.
Everything heals faster than it will. This is being spent without being counted.
You are eighteen. The life begins in earnest.
You graduate from high school. GPA: 2.74. The world is waiting — what comes next… — A practical path. Two years to a certified trade.
The freedom is exactly that: the ability to be somewhere else by Thursday if it seems right.
You are eighteen. The scaffolding of childhood has been removed. The life ahead … — You fix the compass on work and ambition. Other things will have to wait, or happen in the margins.
You are 21. You are studying. The future is still mostly theoretical. This is the beginning of something, though you cannot yet see its shape.
Trade school — year 1 of 2 (electrician).
The body is what it is right now, which you will not appreciate until later.
Which trade will you train in?… — In-demand work. Good pay.
🔧 You complete your electrician certification.
The alley between houses is the social space. The social space is always occupied.
The shopkeeper charges more for the small bag than the large bag costs per unit.… — You know it. It does not change anything.

You bought a coat that is not the cheapest option available. The decision took longer than it should have. The coat is good. You wear it more than any coat before it. You understand now why people talk about coats the way they do.
The corner shop is the shop of last resort and the shop of daily life simultaneously.
The notice is under your door when you come in. Section 21, or its equivalent — … — The search is harder than you expected. You have a month left and two realistic options, both worse than this.

A woman on the platform is crying in the particular way of someone who hoped they could stop by now. She watches the arrivals board. You do not know if she is waiting for someone or has just said goodbye. Either way it is a complete story you have only seen the middle of.
Someone older told you something useful this year. You did not recognise it as useful until later.
You stayed up too late and the morning is paying for it. The payment is manageable.
The pamphlet or the text: drafted and redrafted. The language that says the thing without saying the thing directly.
Something is happening politically in your country. You feel the pull to act.… — You stand for something. The cost is uncertain.

A small child waves at you from a window. You are a stranger to them — you are waving at someone who has not yet learned to need a reason to wave at a stranger. You wave back. The child seems satisfied. The transaction is complete.
The mosquito net arranged before sleep. The arrangement is not decorative.
A friend has a litter of puppies they need to rehome. One of them looks at you w… — Your life immediately acquires a new center of gravity.

Someone is learning to drive in an empty car park — the car moving in careful arcs, too slow, then stopping, then starting again. An instructor in the passenger seat. The driver is concentrating so completely on the wheel that the wheel is the whole world.
The recovery time after illness has quietly extended by a day since your early twenties.
The blood pressure was checked. The number is what it is. You are doing what the number requires.
You are thirty. The life you have been building has become recognizable as a life.
You are sleeping less deeply than you once did. The depth has been changing slowly for years.
You are thirty. The life you have been building has become recognizable as a lif… — The focus narrows. What exists gets deeper rather than wider.

In the park, an elderly man reads on the same bench at the same hour on the same day every week. You have passed him so many times that his absence would register before you understood the absence. He is an anchor. He doesn't know he is an anchor.
Your activism years ago is now part of your record. Someone important has seen i… — You don't apologize for it.
Water in Johannesburg has been limited for weeks. The municipality says it will improve. The improvement has not come. You have learned to use less.
The danfo or the matatu or the shared taxi: the vehicle that runs when it is full and leaves when the driver decides it is full enough.
The dust: in the lungs, in the house, in the ten-year health projection everyone in this town shares.

You queue for six hours. So does the person ahead of you, and the person behind. The line extends further than you can see. People have brought food and umbrellas and folding chairs. When you finally reach the booth it takes less than a minute. On the way out an old man is crying without apparent embarrassment. You understand why.

The first free election. The queues go for miles. People who have never voted in their country stand in them for hours without complaint. The vote cast is the weight of everything that came before it. The transition is not the end of what apartheid did — the land, the wealth, the schools remain organized by the old logic — but it is something new and real happening in a place that needed it.
Mandela is president. The world is watching. Inside the country, the texture of daily life is changing in ways both dramatic and glacially slow. History is happening and so is Tuesday.

You return to the text you grew up with. The same words you memorized as a child mean something different at forty. The passages about loss hit in specific places now. The ones about patience are less abstract. You understand things the child who first read them could not have known yet. The book has not changed. You have.

The internet moves from universities to homes, then to pockets. What information means, what distance means, what a shop is, what a friend is — all of it is changing faster than anyone can name accurately. The rules are being made up as it goes.
April 27, 1994. You queue to vote for the first time. The line stretches for mil… — Seven hours in the heat. You cast your vote. You weep on the way home and don't know why it surprises you.

An old man in a suit on the bus. The suit is old but pressed. The pressing is not nostalgia; it is the standard that was set before you were born and that he has maintained because the standard is still the standard for him. When he gets off the bus he gets off carefully, as if the bus and he have an agreement about the pace of things.
A message arrives from someone you knew before you became whoever you are now. T… — Something returns that you hadn't known was lost.

Your fridge is full. Has been full for years. You still buy more than you need on Friday evenings, still check the expiry dates on things that were bought last week. The habit runs deeper than the reason for it. Your children complain there is never any space. You do not tell them why you need the space taken.

A nurse leaving a hospital at the end of a long shift — not changed out of scrubs, walking to her car. She is carrying the shift with her. You can see this in how she moves: the decision to leave was made but the shift has not entirely ended yet. She is doing the administrative work of returning to herself.
The internet café has opened. The internet is accessed as a destination, not a permanent condition.

The contents of a house being brought out after someone died in it: the furniture, the books, the kitchen things, appearing on the pavement for collection or sale. The objects that accumulated over a lifetime becoming legible now that they are separated from the person who chose them. Someone's taste, visible.
You attended more funerals this year than you did in your thirties. This is not a coincidence.

One lit window in an otherwise dark school building at seven in the evening. A teacher still at a desk. Everyone who is supposed to be here has gone home. The job does not end when the building empties. Whatever is still happening in that room is happening after the official end of what the job requires.
The bus stop is the neighbourhood's common room. You know the regulars by face if not by name.

In the morning, two planes hit the towers. By afternoon, a third hits the Pentagon and a fourth goes down in a Pennsylvania field. The tallest buildings in New York are gone. You watch the footage repeat on television. What follows — the security lines, the databases, the wars — will outlast everyone alive today.

Two people at the next table having an argument in the quiet register of people who have agreed not to make a scene. You can hear the argument without hearing the words. The words are probably not the argument. The argument probably started before dinner and will continue in the car and is inside a longer argument that has been running for some years.
The parent is old now. The parent's oldness puts you on a specific clock.
You did the ordinary thing on the ordinary day and it added up to the year.

You caught a smell this year — something ordinary, something in a shop or a street or another person's kitchen — and for a fraction of a second you were somewhere else entirely, somewhere from your childhood. The specificity of the olfactory memory still surprises you.
The office and the accent and the clothes and the calibration of how much to explain about where you started.
At some point in your forties the back becomes something you negotiate with. The… — The structured approach works. The pain is present but at a level you can manage around.

Through the wall — a thin wall, the kind that older buildings have — you can hear a couple arguing. Not the words, just the shape: the escalation, the pause, the different tone when one of them starts to cry, or to apologise, or both. Then it stops. You go back to whatever you were doing. Behind every wall, someone is living.
The people on the train or the bus that you have seen every morning for years. You know their routines. You do not know their names.

An old man sits alone in the hospital corridor. He is not unwell — his posture is too settled for that, the posture of someone waiting for someone else. He is holding a plastic bag with things in it. He has been here before.
The landlord is a presence in the background, not physically but financially.
A contractor did substandard work that cost you significantly. Your solicitor sa… — The case takes eighteen months. You receive a settlement that partially covers your losses.

You notice a woman in the supermarket buying a single portion of something. She picks it up and then puts it back and takes a different one. It is a very small decision. You understand that it is not a small decision.

The banks are not actually solid. This becomes clear in September. Firms that existed for a century are gone over a weekend. Governments that said there was no money for schools find money for banks. The unemployment numbers rise for years afterward. The people who caused the crash keep their bonuses.

The things people assume you are missing — closeness, the particular warmth of being known — are not entirely absent. They are distributed differently. A friend who has known you for twenty years. The two-hour phone call. The comfort that does not require a shared address. You have assembled something that works. It does not have a name in the language of couplehood. It does not need one.
Something about plenty still feels temporary. You buy more than needed because the need taught you to.
You are fifty. What you carry into this half is mostly set.
You are fifty. You are on your own. You have made something of it. There is more past than there used to be. You are learning to carry it all differently.
What you have here that they don't have there. What they have there that doesn't exist here.
You are fifty. The life you have lived has become visible, like a landscape from… — Not resignation — something quieter. The life is the life. You can live in it now.

You are sitting beside a hospital bed. The person in it is someone from a long time ago — not family, not exactly a friend. They take your hand and they say your name. They say: I would not have made it through that year without you. You do not say anything. There is nothing that needs to be said.
The reading glasses are permanent now. They are the first thing on and the last thing off.

The diagnosis is three years old now. You stopped expecting it to resolve at some point — you cannot mark the exact moment. The condition is no longer a thing that happened to you; it is a parameter of how you live. That transition took longer than you expected, and it was not a single decision.
The clinic is busy. You wait two hours. The doctor is straightforward. Treatment… — The medication dulls the edges but the depression persists underneath.

A person in a park turning their face to the sun with their eyes closed — the particular quality of someone for whom this is not routine pleasure but something else, something they are paying attention to in a way that suggests the sun cannot be taken for granted right now. You don't know their situation. Their face tells you something.
You see the generation in them now — the way their face arrives at your face in the mirror.

Your country signed an agreement it did not cause to be necessary. The wealthy countries that burned coal and oil for two centuries agreed to slow down, somewhat. Your country — which contributed less than one percent of cumulative emissions — will experience the consequences of what the wealthy countries did. The agreement acknowledges this, in language. The reparations promised in that language remain theoretical.

Ten years managing the condition — the specific one, the one with its schedule and its monitoring and its periodic adjustments. You have accumulated a kind of expertise that doctors occasionally ask you to contribute to other patients. You know its rhythms. You know what your body will tell you when something is changing. You did not choose this knowledge. It is yours.

Someone at a grave on what is clearly an anniversary — not a first visit, not a random visit, but the specific visit that comes at this time every year and will come again. They stand there for a while and then they do something small — adjust the flowers, or speak, or just stand. Then they go. They'll be back in a year.
The children do not understand the reference point. They cannot. This is both the success and the distance.

The stories begin with Weinstein and don't stop. The naming of something that was understood but not spoken produces a specific discomfort in some rooms and a specific relief in others. You register both, and what you do with them.
Overtime was offered. The calculation took a moment. You said yes.
You are accounting for the life honestly, the way people do at this age. You did… — You mean it. That is worth something.
The clinic is busy. You wait two hours. The doctor is straightforward. Treatment… — The medication stabilises your condition. You adjust to a new normal.

logEntries.text: Philippines, 1980. The city is enormous and still growing. The neighbourhood you are born into will shape everything that follows.
Your parent disappears into something chemical. The person who returns from it is not quite the same person who left.
A day when the rain makes the decision for you. You stay inside. There is a particular freedom in having the choice removed.

Your parent leaves in the morning. You have not yet learned to hold their absence well. When they come back, you run. This is the rhythm of the day. It will be years before you understand that they are leaving for you.
The children's illness falls to you. The school appointment falls to you. The in-law visit falls to you.
The week ends and the week was, in the main, fine. This is not a small thing.
The same dream keeps coming back. Something is in the dark at the end of the hal… — They sit with you until the room feels safe again. It helps more than you can articulate.
Hunger is a recurring companion. You understand scarcity before you understand much else.
OFW: the family in the house is partly funded by the family not in the house. The remittance is the thread.
Someone appears in your room who nobody else can see. They have a name, preferen… — You set a plate for them at dinner. Your parents smile and say nothing.

The river that runs past the factory has not been right for as long as anyone remembers. The colour changes depending on what they are processing. The company says the discharge is within permitted levels. No one in the village was consulted when the permits were issued.
The corner shop is the shop of last resort and the shop of daily life simultaneously.
The early years end. You begin to know where you are.

You see an adult crying — not at a funeral, not in the context that makes it make sense to you. Just at a table, or on the step outside, in the middle of an ordinary day. Adults cry for reasons you do not yet have. The world is larger than the reasons you know the names of.
The night sounds of the house: sorted and named over years, no longer strange.

An animal you have given a name to dies. On a farm this is not unusual — you were told not to name them, which is why you named it. Someone buries it without ceremony. You are present for the burial. You do not cry, which surprises you, because you thought you would.
Nothing about the day required naming. The day did not mind.
You and a classmate become inseparable. You spend every recess together and they invite you to their birthday party.
The adult who died in the neighbourhood: the way the adults talked quietly about it and stopped when you came near.

You and Remedios Ramos develop a private system — signals, rules, shared language — for navigating the unpredictability at home. You cover for each other. You know things about each other that no one else knows.
The Ramadan nights are longer in some ways than the days — the prayers, the Quran, the particular company of that month.

You wake to the sound of it — the same argument, the same word, the same pause before the door slams. The word is money. You lie still and listen, learning the grammar of what financial pressure does to two people who love each other and are exhausted and afraid.

You need information for a school project. You go to the library. You look in the card catalogue. You find three books and read through all of them to find the two paragraphs you need. The information is imprecise and hard-won. You are more careful with it than you would be if it were easy to find.
The barangay: the political unit small enough to know who you are and keep track.
The body is changing. The world is starting to require something from you.

The library in the late afternoon: each person at a different table, in a different internal world, in a different conversation with something that is not the room. The room holds all of them without asking what they are looking for. This is the arrangement: you come in with your question and the room is indifferent to the question and this is precisely what you needed.
The market is your domain — the price-checking, the weight, the recognition of freshness. The vendor knows you.

Through school, a religious organisation, or your own initiative, you spend time doing something for people outside your immediate circle. It is not glamorous. It is occasionally meaningful in ways that are hard to articulate.
The jeepney is decorated beyond function. The function continues despite the decoration.

The internet moves from universities to homes, then to pockets. What information means, what distance means, what a shop is, what a friend is — all of it is changing faster than anyone can name accurately. The rules are being made up as it goes.
The road to the town: the distance is known in time, not in kilometres. The time depends on the season.
You told one person. One person. The thing you told them — specific, private, no… — They apologize. The apology is real. The friendship ends anyway.
The Quran before sleep: the same verses, the familiar cadence, the transition into rest.

The currency has lost half its value since spring. The savings your family kept in the bank have not changed in number, but what they will buy has. The debt, taken in dollars, has doubled. The government is negotiating with the IMF and the IMF's terms are familiar to anyone who lived through structural adjustment: cut pensions, cut public employment, raise interest rates, open the market.

The baht goes first, then the rupiah, then the won. The savings your family held in the national currency are worth half what they were last month. Companies that existed for decades are gone over a weekend. The IMF arrives with conditions — cut public services, raise interest rates, open the markets — while people are already struggling.
You fall for someone at school. It is consuming and slightly embarrassing.… — They say yes. It lasts a summer, but the feeling stays with you.
The recipe came from someone who is not here to explain what they meant by "a handful."
You are eighteen. The life begins in earnest.
You are old enough to learn to drive. The independence it offers is real.… — The licence opens up the world in practical ways.
You graduate from high school. GPA: 2.46. The world is waiting — what comes next… — A practical path. Two years to a certified trade.
You have a friend from this period who knows a version of you that no one else knows.
You are eighteen. The scaffolding of childhood has been removed. The life ahead … — You fix the compass on work and ambition. Other things will have to wait, or happen in the margins.

A small civil wedding — two people and their witnesses, no family, in good clothes on a Tuesday. The joy is real and it is not the kind of joy that requires an audience. They come out of the registry office and one of them photographs the other against the building. The Tuesday continues around them.
Trade school — year 1 of 2 (electrician).

In the morning, two planes hit the towers. By afternoon, a third hits the Pentagon and a fourth goes down in a Pennsylvania field. The tallest buildings in New York are gone. You watch the footage repeat on television. What follows — the security lines, the databases, the wars — will outlast everyone alive today.
Which trade will you train in?… — In-demand work. Good pay.

In the waiting room, a man reads yesterday's newspaper. A woman adjusts her bag every few minutes. A child has given up asking when they can leave. You are all waiting for different things to happen to you. The room holds everyone's version of the same suspension. Your name is called and you leave them there.
🔧 You complete your electrician certification.
Overtime was offered. The calculation took a moment. You said yes.
The way the phone changed who you can reach and who can reach you: both at once.
Everything necessary is within walking distance or requires the acceptance of a long walk.
Your landlord is not a company. He is the man who lives in the proper-built hous… — It is the right calculation. He is not a bad man. He is a man who owns things.

The departure gate: everyone waiting is going somewhere specific, and the somewhere is specific to each of them in a way the gate cannot accommodate. All the particular arrivals — the house, the hospital, the person who does not know yet that you are coming — held inside the general fact of people waiting for a flight.
Someone your age — a friend, a classmate — died. The impermanence is no longer theoretical.
You meet someone. This feels different from before — more serious, more frighten… — A real relationship begins.

On the street, a woman you do not know and will never see again laughs at something the person with her has said. The laugh is genuine — something in it is not performed. For a moment you are glad she exists, and then she is gone, and you continue.
The year passed in the way that years pass — faster than the days suggested it would.

logEntries.text: Soviet Ukraine, 1985. The maternity ward smells of disinfectant. Your mother was not allowed to have your father in the room.
Some days there is no adult home. Some days there is no food. You learn to manage, long before you should have to.
The road to the town: the distance is known in time, not in kilometres. The time depends on the season.

At night, adults start moving. Something is burning but it is not the kind of burning you can see. Your parents pack a bag and tell you it is temporary. Fifty thousand people leave Pripyat. Most never return. The word 'radiation' enters your vocabulary before you are old enough to know what it means.

Your family shares a kitchen and bathroom with two other families. The schedule for the stove is taped to the wall. Neighbours argue. Adults whisper about things in the hallway. You know every smell, sound, and conflict in this building.
The VCR means the film can be watched again, which changes what watching a film means.

An animal you knew is gone. You were told this in words, but the words haven't matched the reality yet. You go to where it usually was. It isn't there. This is the first time you understand what gone means — not absent, but permanently absent. There is a difference.
The cold requires specific preparation before leaving the house. The preparation is automatic.
The meal was ordinary and the company was good, or the company was ordinary and the meal was good. One or the other.
The specific pleasure of being well after being briefly unwell.

There is a child next door, or in the next flat, or on the same stretch of pavem… — You show them. They do not take it. They look at it the way you wanted them to look at it. Something is agreed without words.

A page that was marks becomes words. You are not sure exactly when it happens — one morning the letters stop being separate things and start being a sentence, and the sentence is about a dog in a field, and the dog is there as clearly as if it were in the room with you. You read it again to make sure.
The calculation of the week's food happens before the buying. The margin between enough and not enough is small.

The wall comes down. The world you were told was fixed — the two sides, the permanent standoff — is not fixed. People are walking through the Brandenburg Gate. History, which felt like a geological fact, is clearly not. Whatever comes next, you are watching the before and after happen in a single night.

The Soviet Union dissolves. Prices double, then double again, within weeks. The salary you were paid last month is no longer what it was worth. The economy your parents understood — the one with fixed prices and guaranteed jobs — no longer exists, and neither does the country.
The early years end. You begin to know where you are.
The same dream keeps coming back. Something is in the dark at the end of the hal… — They sit with you until the room feels safe again. It helps more than you can articulate.
The courtyard between the bloki: the bench, the old women, the children, the version of community that survived.
You and a classmate become inseparable. You spend every recess together and they invite you to their birthday party.
Hyperinflation. Prices change while you queue. You lose a significant portion of your savings to currency collapse.

State-owned enterprises are privatised overnight. A small number of people with the right connections acquire enormous assets at negligible prices. Everyone else watches their savings become worthless as prices are freed and inflation erupts. The word "oligarch" enters the language.
An older child at school has made you their target. It happens daily now.… — You win enough respect to be left alone. Your knuckles ache.
You discover the school library and spend your lunch breaks devouring books.
You eat what is available. What is available is adequate, mostly.

An animal you have given a name to dies. On a farm this is not unusual — you were told not to name them, which is why you named it. Someone buries it without ceremony. You are present for the burial. You do not cry, which surprises you, because you thought you would.
You are memorising things without meaning to: routes, faces, sequences, the way the light falls.

Your grandparent has a silence about one decade that they do not have about other decades. You notice it without understanding it. When you ask about those years, they say something general that covers the years without opening them. The general answer is technically true. The silence around it is more informative.
The CD player has replaced the cassette. The skip protection is still imperfect.
The heating is on. The sound of the radiator is the sound of the season.
The body is changing. The world is starting to require something from you.

On August 17, the government devalues the ruble and defaults on its debt. The ruble loses two-thirds of its value in two weeks. The banks you were told were safe close their doors. The savings you had rebuilt after the early nineties are gone again. Some people joke that they have now lost everything twice. The joke does not last long.
A scholarship application lands in front of you. It would pay for university.… — You get it. The letter arrives on a Tuesday.

The other people your age talk about wanting someone in a way that sounds like a language you have not learned. You listen carefully and try to translate it into something you recognise. You find nothing that matches. You look at the people they describe and feel — not what they describe. You assume you are simply a late developer. You wait.
The dark arrives earlier every day until the solstice. Then it retreats. You are at the part where it is still arriving.
A confrontation that has been building between you and another boy resolves into… — The result is ambiguous. The act is not forgotten.

You go to the city for the first time. The noise. The smell of exhaust and food and people in numbers you have never seen. The buildings are larger than you pictured. You lose your companion in a crowd for twenty minutes and the feeling of it — pure, vertiginous panic — is also a kind of education.
The banya is the social event that is also a health practice that is also a negotiation space.

In the morning, two planes hit the towers. By afternoon, a third hits the Pentagon and a fourth goes down in a Pennsylvania field. The tallest buildings in New York are gone. You watch the footage repeat on television. What follows — the security lines, the databases, the wars — will outlast everyone alive today.
An adult in your community takes a genuine interest in your future.… — The connection reshapes your understanding of what is possible.
You are eighteen. The life begins in earnest.
Military service hangs over every conversation. Your older cousin came back quie… — You buy yourself years. Whether they are the right years remains to be seen.
You graduate from high school. GPA: 2.46. The world is waiting — what comes next… — You earn a partial scholarship and enroll in university.
Broadband has made the internet a permanent fact of the home. It is not turned on and off. It is simply there.
The official tells you there is a fee. There is no official fee. You pay, or you wait forever. This is how things work.
You are eighteen. The scaffolding of childhood has been removed. The life ahead … — You fix the compass on work and ambition. Other things will have to wait, or happen in the margins.
University — year 1 of 4 (healthcare).
The view from the window at the same angle for years. The view has changed slightly. The angle has not.
What will you study at university?… — Demanding, long hours, significant reward.
University — year 2 of 4 (healthcare).
The factory your father worked at for thirty years is being stripped. The machin… — The work is different and often demeaning, but there is work.
University — year 3 of 4 (healthcare).

The banks are not actually solid. This becomes clear in September. Firms that existed for a century are gone over a weekend. Governments that said there was no money for schools find money for banks. The unemployment numbers rise for years afterward. The people who caused the crash keep their bonuses.

The book, or the conversation, or the news cycle of a specific month organises s… — You go to the meeting or the march or the table. The energy of other people who see what you see is real. So is the gap between the energy and the change.

After midnight, one window in the building across the street is still lit. Someone is awake in there for reasons you will never know — working, grieving, nursing an infant, unable to sleep, finishing something, starting something, or simply still awake. The light goes out while you are watching it.
🎓 You graduate from university with a degree in healthcare. The first in your family to do so. Final GPA: 2.60.
The walk to the market, the school, the well. The route is known. The feet know it.
It has been more than a year of this. The low energy that you initially attribut… — Treatment takes months to calibrate. Once it does, the floor is higher. The worst is managed, which is not nothing.
Someone older told you something useful this year. You did not recognise it as useful until later.
The bank approves a personal loan. The interest rate is 18%. The need is real.… — Cash in hand. The repayments begin immediately.
A confrontation at a bar escalates faster than expected. Someone throws a punch.… — You swing.
Bruised ribs, split lip. An expensive lesson.
The photograph is no longer a significant commitment. You take dozens. The dozens accumulate.
You are in the kitchen at an hour you are not usually in the kitchen. The house is quiet. You eat something over the sink. There is a specific freedom in this that has no name.
The smartphone is the last thing checked at night and the first thing checked in the morning. You have noticed this.
The nearest hospital is hours away or the local clinic is understaffed. The diag… — The medication quiets the alarm system enough to live with. Some days are genuinely fine.
Cooking for one involves the repetition of the same three things. The things are fine.
The world agrees to limit warming. Whether it will is a different question from whether it agreed to.
You are thirty. The life you have been building has become recognizable as a life.
The psychiatrist reviews the medication at the annual appointment. The question … — The adjustment takes six weeks to settle. When it does, the year is manageable.
The apartment building's staircase is lit or it is not. You know which it is.
You are thirty. The life you have been building has become recognizable as a lif… — The focus narrows. What exists gets deeper rather than wider.
A parent dies. The grief comes in waves and is never quite finished.
The clinic has Soviet-era equipment, underpaid doctors, and a supply of envelope… — Fast, efficient, expensive. The result is good.
The children who went to the city: you know which households have had a child go. The going is the village's long-term project.
The nearest hospital is hours away or the local clinic is understaffed. The diag… — You complete the programme but relapse within the year. Recovery is rarely linear.
Tetiana Boychuk calls on a Tuesday evening and talks for two hours. The marriage… — You are available through months of it. The friendship deepens in the specific way crisis allows.
In France in 1998, the maternity ward is clean, the forms are in triplicate, and your parents drive home on a road with lane markings.
Hunger is a recurring companion. You understand scarcity before you understand much else.
Your parent hits you. Not once — it becomes the texture of your early years. You learn to read the room before anyone speaks.
The ingredients are available regardless of season now. Winter strawberries exist. You are still deciding how to feel about this.
The apartment is the size it is. You have learned to fit your life into its dimensions.

Late at night the voices in the kitchen rise and then stop. The stopping is worse than the rising. You lie very still and listen to the quality of the silence. You are three or four years old and already an expert in the silence after arguing.
The light pollution means the stars are not visible. You did not notice when you stopped looking for them.
Someone appears in your room who nobody else can see. They have a name, preferen… — You set a plate for them at dinner. Your parents smile and say nothing.

The adults around you do a particular thing at particular times. The bells on Sunday or the sound of the choir reaching into the street. You don't understand it yet, but you've absorbed the rhythm. This sound will feel like the sound of home for a long time.
The phone in your pocket sends a text in the time it once took to compose a letter.

A child watching a funeral from a distance — from a window, or from the edge of the crowd where the adults have not noticed them yet. The child is watching the adults cry. Adults do not usually cry in public. The child is filing this under: things adults do that they normally do not do, which means: things that are serious in a way that ordinary things are not.
The confession booth is a specific appointment. The priest has a specific manner. You know his approach.
The early years end. You begin to know where you are.
You discover the school library and spend your lunch breaks devouring books.
The specific pleasure of being well after being briefly unwell.

Your family is moving. New house, new neighborhood, new school in September. You… — For a while, you do. Then the calls get shorter. Then they stop. Then one resurfaces on social media fifteen years later.
The hymn arrives without prompting. You are doing something else entirely when it appears.
Your father says something small and it lands the wrong way — not cruelty, just carelessness. You carry it out of the room and down the street and further than that.
You are stronger than you were last year. This is verifiable. You have tested it.

The banks are not actually solid. This becomes clear in September. Firms that existed for a century are gone over a weekend. Governments that said there was no money for schools find money for banks. The unemployment numbers rise for years afterward. The people who caused the crash keep their bonuses.
An older child at school has made you their target. It happens daily now.… — You win enough respect to be left alone. Your knuckles ache.
There is a teacher who calls on you when you aren't expecting it. Not to embarrass you — to hear you. You can feel the difference immediately.
A small thing happened that you will not remember in ten years. At the time it occupied you for an afternoon.

Your body is changing and it is doing so without your permission. People notice before you are ready for them to notice. Some of them say things about it. You learn to carry yourself in a particular way, a new way, that you did not have to think about before.
You have started to understand that the people around you will die. This is abstract and yet very clear.
The body is changing. The world is starting to require something from you — and you, more than most, feel the pressure to answer.
The exam: the question you prepared for and the question you did not. The ratio was what the ratio was.

Two people at a nearby table communicate in a language built entirely from incomplete sentences. They have been having this conversation for so long that the nouns are no longer necessary. You watch them finish each other's thoughts without noticing that they are doing it. You wonder what it costs to get there.
Something you did this year was done well. No one acknowledged it. The doing of it acknowledged it.
The university entrance exam is approaching. Everything seems to depend on it.… — The score opens doors you did not know existed.
You looked out the window at a particular moment. What you saw was unremarkable. You looked at it anyway.
Something happens — a test result, a comparison, a public moment — that makes th… — You do not stop. The wound is there but you will not let it stop you. This becomes a pattern.

One hundred and ninety-six countries agree to limit warming to 1.5 degrees. The agreement is binding in intent and voluntary in mechanism. Scientists say it is insufficient. Diplomats say it is historic. Both are true. The planet will warm past 1.5 degrees. The agreement made it somewhat less warm than it would otherwise have been, which is something, and not enough.

The photograph of the boy on the beach travels around the world in hours. Aylan Kurdi, three years old. More than a million people cross into Europe in 2015, most through the Aegean. The political response divides governments. Hungary builds a fence. Germany says wir schaffen das. The debate happening in newspapers and parliaments is conducted almost entirely without asking what made people leave.
Someone posts something unflattering about you online. It's spreading fast.… — The drama escalates briefly, then dies down. People have short memories.
You are eighteen. The proof-of-worth project has a new arena now. The life begins in earnest.
A teacher or authority figure treats you in a way that you know is wrong. Not ab… — They don't love it. You are right and they know it and that doesn't help. But you said it.
The building has its own sounds at night — the pipes, the lift, the street below.

Tarana Burke started the phrase in 2006. It goes everywhere in October 2017 when the Harvey Weinstein stories are published. Something that was understood privately becomes public in a specific week — not the experience, which is not new, but the naming, the scale, the sudden permission to describe what everyone already knew. You have your own version of the accounting that is happening.
You graduate from high school. GPA: 2.76. The world is waiting — what comes next… — You earn a partial scholarship and enroll in university.
You did the ordinary thing on the ordinary day and it added up to the year.
You are eighteen. The scaffolding of childhood has been removed. The life ahead … — You fix the compass on work and ambition. Other things will have to wait, or happen in the margins.

A craftsman at work on something — the hands moving without instruction, the face entirely elsewhere. The object being made is almost incidental. What you are watching is the state of being completely in the right place.
University — year 1 of 4 (healthcare).
The book finished on the commute. The commute provided it. You would not have read it otherwise.
What will you study at university?… — Demanding, long hours, significant reward.

On a bus, a woman across from you takes a photograph from her bag and looks at it for a long time. She is not showing it to anyone. When she puts it away, her face is something you do not have a word for. The bus stops. She gets off. You will never know what was in the photograph.
University — year 2 of 4 (healthcare).

The news in January is about a virus in one city. By March, you are not allowed to leave your home. The streets are empty. The hospitals are not. By the end of the year, more people have died than in any year since the Second World War. Normal resumes slowly and unevenly, and is not the same normal as before.

The doctor sees you the same day. You leave without a bill. This is not remarkable here — it is simply how it works. You have grown up knowing this. You will not understand what it means until you meet someone who grew up without it.
University — year 3 of 4 (healthcare).
🎓 You graduate from university with a degree in healthcare. The first in your family to do so. Final GPA: 2.80.
Food prepared by someone else always tastes differently than food you prepared yourself.
The world moving at a pace that the categories in your head were not designed for.
The tests come back quickly. The specialist explains everything clearly. You hav… — Rehab is hard in ways you did not anticipate. You come out the other side with tools and a fragile clarity.
Someone in the library so absorbed in their book they do not notice you pass. Not performing reading — actually gone somewhere. The door they went through is closed from this side.
A conversation that once required a letter now takes four minutes. The efficiency is not only a gain.
The invitations change shape around this age. What was "come to the party" becom… — The wanting is real. The waiting has its own specific texture.
The news is everywhere and unending and does not require you to seek it out. You have adjusted your seeking.

The city at four in the morning: the people who are awake at four are a subset of the city with its own internal logic. The delivery drivers, the nurses finishing, the insomniacs, the ones who stayed too late at someone else's home. You are briefly among them. Four in the morning makes everyone look like they know something the daytime doesn't.
The office and the accent and the clothes and the calibration of how much to explain about where you started.
When you think about where you came from, it doesn't feel distant. It feels like… — You work harder than anyone expects you to.

A woman sits alone in the church between services. She has not come for the service. The building is available to her in this hour in a way it is not when it is full of purpose. She sits without praying, or with a prayer that has no outward form.
The account was checked without anxiety. This continues to be unusual on a global scale. You know this intermittently.
The table is set for one. This is a fact and not always just a fact.
You are thirty. The proving has been ongoing. You are beginning to notice whether it is working.
The flood that happened in a place that floods did not used to happen and the silence afterward about why.
You have built something demonstrable by now — a career, a record, a reputation … — You commit. The other path closes, as paths do when you stop looking at them.

There is a neighbour you have never spoken to beyond the nod of recognition. You have nodded at each other for years. The relationship is complete without words. If they moved, you would notice the absence.
You know people who have died. The list is longer than it was at thirty.

The Great Barrier Reef is declared functionally extinct by the scientific bodies that track such things. It took 25 million years to form. It bleached to death in under a century. The thing that killed it — carbon dioxide warming the oceans — is still accumulating in the atmosphere. The reef is a specific loss: one species, one ecosystem, one measure of what was possible before.
You are thirty. The scoreboard you have been keeping — achievements, titles, the… — The focus narrows. What exists gets deeper rather than wider.

You woke at three in the morning with a thought that did not warrant waking at three in the morning. The thought knows no proportionality. You lay with it for an hour. It dissolved into sleep before it resolved.

There is an object that has been on the same shelf for fifteen years. You stopped noticing it around year three. This year you noticed it again — by accident, in a particular light. It is still there. It has outlasted the reason you kept it.
The hour at which the working day ends has become uncertain in a way the beginning never was.

There is a book on your bedside table or your shelf that you have been about to read for two years. You are not sure why you haven't. The presence of the unread book is a small persistent fact of the life of this decade.
A parent dies. The grief comes in waves and is never quite finished.
The reading glasses appeared this year, or last year, or are imminent. The print that was fine is now borderline.
You and Louis Richard have become people who exchange birthday messages. That is… — You call instead of text. The conversation runs long in a way neither of you planned.

An old man in a suit on the bus. The suit is old but pressed. The pressing is not nostalgia; it is the standard that was set before you were born and that he has maintained because the standard is still the standard for him. When he gets off the bus he gets off carefully, as if the bus and he have an agreement about the pace of things.
The parent is old now. The parent's oldness puts you on a specific clock.

In a restaurant, a couple sits without speaking. They are not angry — or not visibly angry. They have eaten most of their meals together for a long time and the silence has a quality not available to strangers. You do not know if the silence is comfortable or the end of something.
The smell of something cooking is the first announcement that someone is home.

The proof has accumulated. You have what you worked for. The question that surfaces at forty, quietly, is whether the accumulation has addressed the original doubt. You have a feeling you already know the answer.

Through the wall — a thin wall, the kind that older buildings have — you can hear a couple arguing. Not the words, just the shape: the escalation, the pause, the different tone when one of them starts to cry, or to apologise, or both. Then it stops. You go back to whatever you were doing. Behind every wall, someone is living.
The pandemic and the years after the pandemic and the way the years after the pandemic feel like the pandemic didn't end so much as change register.

You remembered a dream into the afternoon, which is unusual. Most dreams leave by nine. This one stayed. You are not sure what it means and are not inclined to interpret it formally, but it occupied a specific corner of the day.
The apéritif is not optional. It is the transition between the work and the evening.

The television shows camps at the borders of your country. Climate refugees — though the law does not call them that. The argument about what to do with them has been going for twenty years. It is still going. The camps are permanent. Children are being born in them who have never been anywhere else.

A box of old photographs for sale in a market — strangers at their best moments, birthdays, graduations, days at the beach. You go through several of them. Every person in them was someone's whole world at the time the photograph was taken.
There is something you are putting off. You know this. You are still putting it off.

The Arctic Ocean is ice-free in summer for the first time in recorded human history — and, scientists say, for the first time in at least three million years. The news is treated as a milestone. Scientists resist the framing of milestone: a milestone implies a journey with a destination, and this is not that kind of journey. This is a door. It opens once.
A junior colleague is struggling with a presentation — work you both contributed… — They present it well. The room applauds. You clap. Nobody names what this cost you, because nobody knows.

Passing the hospital at night, you look up at the lit windows. They look like any other lit windows except that the people behind them did not choose to be there tonight. The different kind of night that those rooms contain goes on behind glass, visible and separate.
The first holiday after: the day is the same day and also not. You understood why people said it would be different.

A man sitting in a parked car outside a building, not going in. His hands are on the wheel. He is not going anywhere. The car is parked and he has been sitting in it for some time. When he finally gets out he gets out the way a person gets out when they have decided that going in is what comes next.
A person who used to be young is now the age you were when you thought of them as young. This happens continuously.
The cleaner comes on a scheduled day. Their name is known. The arrangement is professional and also human.

Scientists confirm that the West Antarctic Ice Sheet has entered an irreversible collapse. The process will take centuries, but it has begun and it cannot now be stopped. Sea level rise of one to three metres is now locked in regardless of what happens to emissions from this point. The news arrives on a Tuesday. People discuss it and go back to what they were doing. This is perhaps the most important thing that has happened in human history and it is received like weather.
A message arrives from someone you knew before you became whoever you are now. T… — Something returns that you hadn't known was lost.

A heat dome sits over the region for three weeks. The hospitals are overwhelmed. Most of the dead are elderly and alone. The word unprecedented is used again. It is no longer, technically, unprecedented — this is the fourth such event in twelve years — but the word keeps appearing in the official statements as if repetition does not affect its meaning.
The recipe came from someone who is not here to explain what they meant by "a handful."
Families are arriving from the coast, from the delta regions, from the islands. … — You give what you can. It is not nothing. It is also not enough, and you know this while you give it.
You are fifty. The proof-of-worth is what it is. The question of what it was for is not going away.

You are fifty. You are on your own. You have made something of it. The drive to prove yourself is still present. You have gotten somewhat better at noticing when it runs you and asking whether that is necessary. You are learning to carry it all differently.
You are fifty. What you have made of yourself — is that the word for it? What it… — Not resignation — something quieter. The life is the life. You can live in it now.

A person in a park turning their face to the sun with their eyes closed — the particular quality of someone for whom this is not routine pleasure but something else, something they are paying attention to in a way that suggests the sun cannot be taken for granted right now. You don't know their situation. Their face tells you something.
The argument about the right way to do something is itself a form of attention paid to quality.

The family you helped is five years settled. The eldest child goes to school here now. You receive a message once a year, at Eid or Christmas or New Year depending on the year and the sender's mood. It is a small thing. It persists, which is more than you expected.
Someone says their name in conversation — a different person, same name — and for a fraction of a second your body does not know the difference.
Faith has become something different — less about rules and more about comfort in the face of time.
The reading glasses are permanent now. They are the first thing on and the last thing off.
The way the conversation moved past you before you finished the sentence. This began at fifty and has not changed.
You have spent a life demonstrating something. At fifty-five, the question arriv… — Not every question gets an answer. The life was the life. You can stop arguing with it.

A levy upgrade is being proposed. A council meeting on flood risk management. The engineer presents models. The question is not whether the water will come higher but how much it will cost to keep it out and who pays. The answer, as these things go, is: everyone, unevenly.
The view from the window at the same angle for years. The view has changed slightly. The angle has not.
The diagnosis arrives at your regular check-up. Your glucose has been creeping f… — Within six months the readings are stable. The condition is part of your daily routine now.

Someone across the room makes a gesture you recognise — a particular way of carrying weight, the slight turn of the head when listening. It takes a moment to understand: it is the gesture you make. This person you have never met moves as you move. You wonder if either of you learned it or arrived at it separately.
Their handwriting is still in things. A note inside a book, a label on something at the back of a cupboard.
Bolivia, 1981. The country is in motion — politically, economically, always. You arrive to a family of modest means but solid foundations.
A childhood illness passes through your area. You survive, but it leaves its mark.
The week had a middle and an end and the end arrived.

The world is full of things to climb and fall from. You are learning both. A knee scraped on stone. The way height looks from somewhere you're not supposed to be. The way your body is more resilient than you expected, and sometimes less.
The sound of the adults talking in the next room while you fall asleep.
Hyperinflation. Prices change while you queue. You lose a significant portion of your savings to currency collapse.
The weekend is real — two days in which the body does something other than what it is told.

You do not know the word for your situation yet. You know that some children at the edge of your world have things you don't. You know that the adults manage their faces when certain subjects come up. You are storing this without being able to say what it is.
The rosary at the end of the day. The beads are known by touch. The words arrive without thought.
Your father leaves before it is light. A bag goes with him. Your mother tells yo… — You are told something. The answer does not answer the question. You stop asking, which is its own kind of answer.
The dry season is hot in a way the body eventually accepts as ordinary.

The teacher asks the class a question. You know the answer — you have known it for a week. She looks around the room and calls on someone else. When you answer anyway, she looks briefly surprised. You file this away. You will carry it for a long time.
The feast day of the patron saint of the town is the year's social calendar compressed into one day.
The early years end. You begin to know where you are.

There is a child next door, or in the next flat, or on the same stretch of pavem… — You show them. They do not take it. They look at it the way you wanted them to look at it. Something is agreed without words.
You win a drawing competition at school. Your entry is pinned on the wall for three weeks. You are given something small to mark it. You carry it home with both hands.
A bruise appeared on your knee without you remembering how it arrived. This happens frequently.
Learning to read: the moment the marks become words is something you do not remember happening. And then they did.
The radio is playing military music. That is how you know. No normal broadcast, just the march. The streets are quiet in ways that are not calm.

The river that runs past the factory has not been right for as long as anyone remembers. The colour changes depending on what they are processing. The company says the discharge is within permitted levels. No one in the village was consulted when the permits were issued.
The cholitas in the market: the bowler hat, the layered skirt, the manner of women who carry substantial weight on their backs and know it.
You spend every recess with the same person. You have a shared language now — re… — They come. Your bedroom becomes a world you've built together.

There are sixty children in the classroom. One teacher, one board, no textbooks — only the notebook you share with your cousin. The teacher writes on the board and you copy. You have been doing this since you were old enough to hold a pencil. You do not know yet that this is unusual.

An old person tells you something about what the world was like when they were young. The world they describe does not exist anymore. You understand, without having words for it yet, that you are talking to someone from a different time — and that this is strange and ordinary at once.
Saturday morning is a different quality of morning. The quality is something close to freedom.
The harvest: the days when everything else stops because this cannot wait.
The body is changing. The world is starting to require something from you.
You did the ordinary thing on the ordinary day and it added up to the year.
You fall for someone at school. It is consuming and slightly embarrassing.… — They say yes. It lasts a summer, but the feeling stays with you.

You start keeping a record of things — thoughts, what happened, the shape of a feeling you are trying to hold in place. You don't call it anything. You hide it, not because it contains anything dangerous, but because the private nature of it is part of what it is.
The person who sits next to you in class is neither friend nor stranger. This category is very crowded at this age.
The question of who you are becomes urgent. The answers available to you are mos… — The friction disappears. Something quieter replaces it.
The view from the window at the same angle for years. The view has changed slightly. The angle has not.
There is a person. You are aware of them in a way that has no useful precedent i… — You say something. It doesn't come out the way you rehearsed. It doesn't need to.

You are stopped on the way home by two officers. Your friend — who walked five steps ahead of you — was not stopped. You know why. The officers find nothing and wave you on. The conversation your parent has with you that evening is called "the talk" in every household that looks like yours.
The children who went to the city: you know which households have had a child go. The going is the village's long-term project.
You find yourself drawn to making something. The feeling is specific — not a gen… — The instrument becomes the place you go when language fails.
You are eighteen. The life begins in earnest.
The way the phone changed who you can reach and who can reach you: both at once.

In 1999, Bolivia privatised Cochabamba's water supply as a condition of an IMF loan. Aguas del Tunari, a subsidiary of Bechtel, raised prices by an average of 50 percent — in a city where the median worker earned about $100 a month. In January 2000 the city rose: roadblocks, strikes, a general assembly that occupied the city center. In April the government declared a state of siege. Victor Hugo Daza, age 17, was shot dead by security forces. Bechtel left. The water returned to public hands. It was called the first successful uprising against water privatisation in the world.
The official tells you there is a fee. There is no official fee. You pay, or you wait forever. This is how things work.
You graduate from high school. GPA: 2.46. The world is waiting — what comes next… — A practical path. Two years to a certified trade.

In the morning, two planes hit the towers. By afternoon, a third hits the Pentagon and a fourth goes down in a Pennsylvania field. The tallest buildings in New York are gone. You watch the footage repeat on television. What follows — the security lines, the databases, the wars — will outlast everyone alive today.
You are eighteen. The scaffolding of childhood has been removed. The life ahead … — You fix the compass on work and ambition. Other things will have to wait, or happen in the margins.
A practical assessment at trade school reveals you have a real aptitude for this work.
Trade school — year 1 of 2 (electrician).
Which trade will you train in?… — In-demand work. Good pay.

A small child waves at you from a window. You are a stranger to them — you are waving at someone who has not yet learned to need a reason to wave at a stranger. You wave back. The child seems satisfied. The transaction is complete.
🔧 You complete your electrician certification.
The menu is read from right to left, always. You know this is the direction you learned it.
