In September–October 2003, protests erupt across Bolivia over a government plan to export natural gas — the second-largest reserves in South America — via a pipeline through Chile to a Pacific port. For Bolivians, routing the pipeline through Chile reopens the wound of the 1884 War of the Pacific, which cost Bolivia its coastal access. Indigenous Aymara communities in El Alto blockade roads. The army opens fire on protestors in the neighbourhood of Warisata. Sixty-seven people die. President Gonzalo Sánchez de Lozada, known as Goni, flees to Miami. The gas stays in Bolivian soil.

At eight in the evening, one office in the office building across the way is still lit. A figure at a desk, visible in silhouette. You are leaving. They are still there. You do not know if they are getting ahead or being left behind. Both can look the same from outside.
The packed lunch is the economics of the day: made at home, eaten at work, nothing spent on a meal.

A family outside a prison before visiting hours: children dressed carefully, a woman holding a bag of things that will be inspected. The waiting is practiced — they have done this before. When the door opens, the children go in and their faces change. They have learned to be whoever they need to be in there.
You have watched three people from the village leave. You know what they have now and what they no longer have.

On December 18, 2005, Evo Morales wins the Bolivian presidential election with 54 percent of the vote — the first outright majority in Bolivian electoral history. An Aymara cocalero from the Chapare, Morales becomes the first indigenous person to hold the presidency of a country where more than 60 percent of the population identifies as indigenous. He takes office on January 22, 2006, in a ceremony that incorporates Aymara spiritual rituals at Tiwanaku before the formal inauguration at Palacio de Quemado.

You missed the train by thirty seconds. You watched it leave. The next one is in an hour. You find a place to sit and think about what you would have done with that hour had it been given to you. You do the thing you thought of.
Sunday is the day with a different shape. The getting-ready, the walk or drive, the greeting of people seen only in this context.

A stranger on the street is delighted by something that has nothing to do with you — a message, a thought, something remembered. Their face does something private and they do not try to explain it to the street. The joy is entirely self-contained. You catch it briefly, like catching a scent.
The road to the town: the distance is known in time, not in kilometres. The time depends on the season.
You are in the kitchen at an hour you are not usually in the kitchen. The house is quiet. You eat something over the sink. There is a specific freedom in this that has no name.
You stayed up too late and the morning is paying for it. The payment is manageable.

The banks are not actually solid. This becomes clear in September. Firms that existed for a century are gone over a weekend. Governments that said there was no money for schools find money for banks. The unemployment numbers rise for years afterward. The people who caused the crash keep their bonuses.
The body is learning what it can do. The learning is mostly good news.

The interest rate is lower than you feared. The monthly payment is manageable. You sign at three places and walk out with what you needed. The loan officer says congratulations. The weight of it arrives later — not all at once, but as a low-grade awareness that some of your future income already has an owner.
The seasons are the years. The year is measured from planting to harvest to the dry to the rains.
the city at night from the bus window is more light than you have ever seen in o… — The cousin's contact is real and unexpectedly generous. You sleep on a floor but you are not alone in it.
The children do not understand the reference point. They cannot. This is both the success and the distance.
You are thirty. The life you have been building has become recognizable as a life.

You know which aisle has what. You know which product is never in stock on a Monday. You know the checkout queue to avoid. This knowledge, accumulated without effort over years of the same loop, is the kind of intelligence that goes unacknowledged. The weekly shop takes twenty minutes less than it used to. You have absorbed the shop.
You are thirty. The life you have been building has become recognizable as a lif… — The focus narrows. What exists gets deeper rather than wider.
The parent is old now. The parent's oldness puts you on a specific clock.

There is a neighbour you have never spoken to beyond the nod of recognition. You have nodded at each other for years. The relationship is complete without words. If they moved, you would notice the absence.

You see them across a room at a wedding. Your first love. They look the same and… — The conversation is short and warm and slightly disorienting. You leave it where it is. That version of you was a different person. This one is better.
The confession booth is a specific appointment. The priest has a specific manner. You know his approach.

Your country signed an agreement it did not cause to be necessary. The wealthy countries that burned coal and oil for two centuries agreed to slow down, somewhat. Your country — which contributed less than one percent of cumulative emissions — will experience the consequences of what the wealthy countries did. The agreement acknowledges this, in language. The reparations promised in that language remain theoretical.

You are among hundreds of thousands crossing the Mediterranean in an inflatable boat. The crossing takes hours. Some boats sink. Whether you reach land, and which land, and what happens when you do — these are decided by wind and coast guards and the politics of countries that are not yours.

There is an object that has been on the same shelf for fifteen years. You stopped noticing it around year three. This year you noticed it again — by accident, in a particular light. It is still there. It has outlasted the reason you kept it.
The nearest hospital is hours away or the local clinic is understaffed. The diag… — The medication quiets the alarm system enough to live with. Some days are genuinely fine.

You catch yourself dreaming in the new language. The accent is still there — it will always be there, to some people — but it is softer than it was. A shopkeeper guesses a different country from the right one. Your old friends back home notice when you call: the cadence has changed. You are becoming someone they recognize less. You are not sure how you feel about this.
The bus stop is the neighbourhood's common room. You know the regulars by face if not by name.

A nurse leaving a hospital at the end of a long shift — not changed out of scrubs, walking to her car. She is carrying the shift with her. You can see this in how she moves: the decision to leave was made but the shift has not entirely ended yet. She is doing the administrative work of returning to herself.
The nearest hospital is hours away or the local clinic is understaffed. The diag… — The medication lifts the fog enough to function. It is not happiness, but it is solid ground.
In United Kingdom in 1953, the maternity ward is clean, the forms are in triplicate, and your parents drive home on a road with lane markings.

Someone takes photographs of you. You will see them later — formal poses, an expression you won't remember making. You will not remember the moment of being photographed. Only the photograph. This is the first evidence of yourself that you will have.
The feast day of the patron saint of the town is the year's social calendar compressed into one day.
One of your parents drinks. You understand what drunk looks like before you understand much else. The smell is particular. The unpredictability is worse.
There is a word you have been practising. You say it when you can.
The subsidy: the form, the compliance, the policy that changed and the adjustment to the policy.

Your grandparent keeps you while your parents work. The day has a different texture — slower, full of a kind of attention you don't get elsewhere. There are stories about before you existed. You don't understand all of them. You keep them anyway.
The single tree that marks the year — the same tree in the same position, season after season.
The same dream keeps coming back. Something is in the dark at the end of the hal… — They sit with you until the room feels safe again. It helps more than you can articulate.
Sleep comes easily. The night is not complicated yet.

A page that was marks becomes words. You are not sure exactly when it happens — one morning the letters stop being separate things and start being a sentence, and the sentence is about a dog in a field, and the dog is there as clearly as if it were in the room with you. You read it again to make sure.
The early years end. You begin to know where you are.
Someone appears in your room who nobody else can see. They have a name, preferen… — You set a plate for them at dinner. Your parents smile and say nothing.

The lights go out in the evening, and the next morning too. Your parent is on the phone for a long time using a voice you know means something is wrong. The electricity company has cut the service. Your neighbour lets you charge a torch from their socket. For two weeks you do your homework by the light of it.

You and Olivia Martin develop a private system — signals, rules, shared language — for navigating the unpredictability at home. You cover for each other. You know things about each other that no one else knows.
The choice is between two things of similar quality. The price is not the deciding factor.

At an adult gathering, a child of about seven who has found a corner and is conducting a private game with small objects from their pocket. The adults are talking about adult things and the child has built a complete world two feet from the floor, entirely self-sufficient, requiring nothing from the conversation above it.
The cookbook was opened. Something new was made. This happens occasionally.

For thirteen days, the adults are frightened in a way they cannot hide. The news is terse. The drills at school become something different. Then it passes and no one explains what would have happened.

The neighbour's house is lit in the evenings and you can see the shapes of people moving through the rooms. They do not know you are watching. Something about lit-up houses belonging to people you do not know is interesting in a way you do not yet have a word for.
The rain is neither unusual nor over. It is the condition.

At school you practice the drill: when the bell rings, you go under the desk and cover your head with your arms. The civil defense leaflet your family received explains what to do in case of attack. Your parents keep it in a drawer. At a certain point you understand that the desk would not protect you from anything and that everyone knows this, and that the drill is for something other than survival — it is for the management of a fear that has no practical resolution.
The energy at 10am on a Saturday morning is a specific quantity the adult body will spend decades missing.

There is something you notice about yourself that you do not have a word for yet. It is not like what the boys talk about in the locker room or what the girls whisper about during lunch. You do not know if it is wrong or simply different. You file it somewhere deep and return to it at night.
The body is changing. The world is starting to require something from you.
The grey sky is the default sky. The sun, when it arrives, causes comment.
There is a person. You are aware of them in a way that has no useful precedent i… — You say something. It doesn't come out the way you rehearsed. It doesn't need to.
The donation was made. The amount was decided in a moment without significant calculation.
There is someone you cannot stop thinking about. This is not new — you have felt… — You know. You carry it carefully for now.

You are out at night, later than you have been out before, without adults who are responsible for you. The streets feel different at this hour. Your friends are different too — louder, more deliberate, performing something. You are not sure if you are performing too. You think probably yes.
The year passed in the way that years pass — faster than the days suggested it would.
You stay out past the time you were supposed to be home. Not by much the first t… — The punishment is real. So is the relief of having it handled.
The weather and the price and the yield: three variables that do not coordinate.

Fifty thousand women march down Fifth Avenue in New York. You watch the news footage or read the coverage. Something is shifting in how the question is being asked — not whether women deserve equality but when and by what means. The answer to the first question is no longer in dispute in the room you grew up in. The second question is being answered in the streets.
The ingredients are available regardless of season now. Winter strawberries exist. You are still deciding how to feel about this.
You are eighteen. The life begins in earnest.
The question of who you are becomes urgent. The answers available to you are mos… — The friction disappears. Something quieter replaces it.
The table is set for one. This is a fact and not always just a fact.
You graduate from high school. GPA: 2.76. The world is waiting — what comes next… — You earn a partial scholarship and enroll in university.
The garden is the ongoing project that will not be finished. This is not a failure of the garden.

OPEC announces an oil embargo. The price of oil quadruples in weeks. Petrol queues stretch around blocks. In some countries the government introduces odd-even rationing. The relationship between the world and the ground beneath it has changed.
Arab oil producers embargo the West. Fuel lines stretch around blocks. The postwar prosperity consensus cracks.
You are eighteen. The scaffolding of childhood has been removed. The life ahead … — You fix the compass on work and ambition. Other things will have to wait, or happen in the margins.
University — year 1 of 4 (healthcare).
The hangover lasts one day, not two. This seems correct and will not always be true.
What will you study at university?… — Demanding, long hours, significant reward.

After midnight, one window in the building across the street is still lit. Someone is awake in there for reasons you will never know — working, grieving, nursing an infant, unable to sleep, finishing something, starting something, or simply still awake. The light goes out while you are watching it.
University — year 2 of 4 (healthcare).
The West Indies win. The team from the Caribbean, drawn from islands where cricket was a gift of the British Empire, has become the best team on earth. The empire's game now belongs to someone else.
The panic attacks started six months ago. A GP refers you to a mental health ass… — CBT helps. The progress is slow and measurable. You begin to understand the pattern.

You have listened to the same song too many times to count this year. The number does not seem possible given how often you wanted it again. The song has gone into the layer of things you will associate with this period for the rest of your life.
University — year 3 of 4 (healthcare).
The pub is the third space — not home, not work, but the other one.

A blind man navigating this street so quickly and accurately that he is overtaking people who can see. He knows this route the way the body knows things — through repetition until the knowledge is stored somewhere more reliable than sight. You step aside. He does not need the gesture but you make it anyway.
🎓 You graduate from university with a degree in healthcare. Final GPA: 2.79.
You are ill for three days. The illness passes. The body reasserts itself.

The thought arrives fully formed: what if the thing you sent — the email, the message, the thing you said in the meeting — was misread. What if the way it landed is not the way you intended. The thought runs through the possible interpretations twice and then a third time, each time less charitable. You go back and re-read it. It reads fine. The thought does not trust this assessment and runs the loop again.

A man working in the street — a repair, a delivery, an installation — is singing to himself. The song is not for anyone. He does not know you are hearing it. The sound of someone singing who does not know they are being heard is one of the most private sounds there is.
You are running or exercising because you want to, or because you have started to think about what happens if you don't.

65 countries do not go to the Moscow Olympics. The USA boycott, in response to the Soviet invasion of Afghanistan, takes their athletes with it. The athletes who spent four years preparing for this have their preparation rendered irrelevant by a diplomatic decision that neither side believes will change anything. The Soviet Union invades Afghanistan regardless. The athletes train anyway, for the 1984 Games, which the Soviet bloc then boycotts in return. Both boycotts accomplish nothing except the cancellation of four years of human effort.

The departure gate: everyone waiting is going somewhere specific, and the somewhere is specific to each of them in a way the gate cannot accommodate. All the particular arrivals — the house, the hospital, the person who does not know yet that you are coming — held inside the general fact of people waiting for a flight.
The menu is read from right to left, always. You know this is the direction you learned it.

A disease with no name at first, then a name without a treatment. The deaths are concentrated in gay men, intravenous drug users, hemophiliacs. The government is slow to respond. By the time effective treatment arrives, a generation of men has been hollowed out of certain cities. You knew someone. Then you knew several people. Then you stopped counting.
A cooking class, a gifted cookbook, or simply the need to feed yourself better. … — The flavours are memory itself. Others gather around your table.
The confession booth is a specific appointment. The priest has a specific manner. You know his approach.
You are thirty. The life you have been building has become recognizable as a life.
You stopped going to the thing. Not because you decided to stop — you simply did… — You go. It is difficult and also ordinary. The relief of having gone is almost physical.

The National Union of Mineworkers has called a strike against pit closures. It will last a year. The government has prepared — coal stockpiled, police deployed in numbers the coalfields have never seen. The communities built around the pits — County Durham, South Wales, Yorkshire — are choosing sides, and the choice cuts through families. There is no clean position.
You are thirty. The life you have been building has become recognizable as a lif… — The focus narrows. What exists gets deeper rather than wider.

A pilgrim on the road. Not at the destination — on the road between. They have been walking for some days. The walking has a quality that ordinary walking does not have, which is that it is both the means and the point. When they get to where they are going, the getting there will be less important than the road that produced it.
You understand now what your parents were going through. The understanding arrived late. It is still useful.

An old friend from school reaches out. They found you through something — a mutu… — The first hour is performance. The second hour is something closer to real. You leave with their number and a faint grief for the decades that happened differently for each of you.

At the market, an old woman is arranging her goods at the same stall where, you realise, she has probably been arranging them every morning for decades. The arrangement is exact and without hurry. The decades are in the arrangement.
The cleaner comes on a scheduled day. Their name is known. The arrangement is professional and also human.

There is an object that has been on the same shelf for fifteen years. You stopped noticing it around year three. This year you noticed it again — by accident, in a particular light. It is still there. It has outlasted the reason you kept it.
Some of the things you worried about did not happen. Others happened and were survived. The accounting is ongoing.

You spend part of the day doing something no one else will ever know you did. The repair. The filing. The reply that needed to go out. When it is done there is no record of its undone state, which means no one can see that it is now done. You notice this. You do it anyway.
The anxiety that the current condition will reverse is older than the current condition.

A nurse leaving a hospital at the end of a long shift — not changed out of scrubs, walking to her car. She is carrying the shift with her. You can see this in how she moves: the decision to leave was made but the shift has not entirely ended yet. She is doing the administrative work of returning to herself.

The wall comes down. The world you were told was fixed — the two sides, the permanent standoff — is not fixed. People are walking through the Brandenburg Gate. History, which felt like a geological fact, is clearly not. Whatever comes next, you are watching the before and after happen in a single night.

There is an old man who sits in front of the same building at the same time every day. You have passed him so many times that he has become part of the landscape. One day he is not there. He will not be there again. You do not know his name. You find that you notice his absence longer than you would have expected.
The mobile phone is the size of a small brick and calls cost significantly. You use it for genuine emergencies.
The week has a texture to it that the years in your twenties did not have. Monday feels like Monday.

Two people at the next table having an argument in the quiet register of people who have agreed not to make a scene. You can hear the argument without hearing the words. The words are probably not the argument. The argument probably started before dinner and will continue in the car and is inside a longer argument that has been running for some years.
The building has its own sounds at night — the pipes, the lift, the street below.
You are 38. Most of the people you know have been with someone for a long time n… — You decide not to stop hoping for it. This takes a particular kind of courage at this age.
You are forty. Half of what you intended, more or less. You have stopped apologising for the gap between what you meant and what is.
The smell of something cooking is the first announcement that someone is home.

The back corner of the yard goes to seed one spring. The following year you buy seeds from the market. It becomes a thing you do in the mornings, before the day claims you. The plants do not know or care what else is happening. This turns out to be why you do it.
You went somewhere and came back and the somewhere and the coming back were both ordinary.

A day with nothing required. You feel the shape of the nothing and it is unusual — not comfortable at first, then more comfortable. By afternoon you have found the thing you actually wanted to do with a free day, not the thing you had told yourself you would do if you had time. The two are different. The distance between them is information.
You have thought, more than once this year, about your own end — not with fear, with arithmetic.
You have started using a phrase you did not use before. You do not know exactly when you started. It sounds, to you, like something a person slightly older would say. You are slightly older.

You have done things that people with partners don't often do: moved for a job without negotiation, quit a job without discussion, spent three months somewhere impractical, changed your life in ways that required only your own agreement. The freedom is real and you have used it. Alongside it, less discussable, is the knowledge that no one has really witnessed the accumulation of your life from close proximity. You are known by many people in pieces, and by no one entirely.
A friend has done well. Not spectacularly — no headlines — but the house, the jo… — The envy passes. What remains is the friendship.

There is a book on your bedside table or your shelf that you have been about to read for two years. You are not sure why you haven't. The presence of the unread book is a small persistent fact of the life of this decade.
The charitable donation: the question of where it goes and whether it goes well.

In the park, an elderly man reads on the same bench at the same hour on the same day every week. You have passed him so many times that his absence would register before you understood the absence. He is an anchor. He doesn't know he is an anchor.
You grew up with nothing and are not now with nothing, and this is the story you do not know how to fully tell.

Six months after the friendship ended, you start to see it clearly. The small put-downs that you explained away at the time. The way every conversation circled back to them. The competitiveness that ran underneath everything. Distance is doing what closeness couldn't — it is giving you an honest view. You feel relief and then feel guilty about the relief.

In the morning, two planes hit the towers. By afternoon, a third hits the Pentagon and a fourth goes down in a Pennsylvania field. The tallest buildings in New York are gone. You watch the footage repeat on television. What follows — the security lines, the databases, the wars — will outlast everyone alive today.
The children do not understand the reference point. They cannot. This is both the success and the distance.
You are fifty. What you carry into this half is mostly set.

In the weeks after the attacks, someone says something — at work, at a dinner ta… — The laughter stops. The mood changes. You have made enemies of people you will see every day. You find you can live with that.
You are fifty. You are on your own. You have made something of it. There is more past than there used to be. You are learning to carry it all differently.
You are tired in the afternoon in a way that is different from being tired after not sleeping.
You are fifty. The life you have lived has become visible, like a landscape from… — Not resignation — something quieter. The life is the life. You can live in it now.
People come to you for advice. You have become, without noticing it, an elder.
The neighbour's farm that was sold. The neighbour before that. The size of the holdings growing around the ones that remain.
The doctor gives it a name. It is a name you have heard before — on television, … — The condition is manageable when managed. This is what manageable means.
The garage contains things that are not cars. This is the function of the garage.

You have known this person for years in a peripheral way — a neighbor, a fellow member of something. One afternoon, for a small reason, a conversation goes further than it should. Two hours pass. You go home and think about it. Something about being older makes it possible to let someone in quickly, or perhaps it is only that you have fewer reasons left to be careful.

The banks are not actually solid. This becomes clear in September. Firms that existed for a century are gone over a weekend. Governments that said there was no money for schools find money for banks. The unemployment numbers rise for years afterward. The people who caused the crash keep their bonuses.
You think about what you leave behind. The question has an answer, though you ar… — The act of writing clarifies what mattered.

At the memorial at seven in the morning, one man. He is in his eighties and he has brought flowers. He stands there longer than the flowers require. He came at this hour so that he would be alone, which means that you, having come at this hour for your own reasons, are an intrusion. You leave him to it.

Looking back, you can trace the shape of it through the decades. The years it took from you. The years you got back. The things you understand about yourself because of it that you would not understand without it — not a gift, not a lesson, not something you would recommend, but a knowing. You carry it differently now than you did at thirty.
You are going through things — objects, papers, photographs. Some of it is necessary. Some is just looking.
The week had a middle and an end and the end arrived.

You wake at four in the morning and do not go back to sleep. It is not anxiety, not any particular thought — just the body announcing a new schedule. You lie there for a while. Then you get up. The house is dark and quiet. The hours between four and six are yours in a way daytime hours rarely are.
The photograph is no longer a significant commitment. You take dozens. The dozens accumulate.
The tests come back quickly. The specialist explains everything clearly. You hav… — The medication lifts the fog enough to function. It is not happiness, but it is solid ground.
WhatsApp has replaced the phone call as default communication. The voice note is somewhere between a call and a text.
You book the tickets for somewhere you have been meaning to go for thirty years.… — The trip takes longer than it would have and leaves you more tired. You remember most of it in detail.

A friend your own age dies. You have known each other for forty years, or close to it. The arithmetic changes: the people who knew you when you were young are fewer now. Each one of them carried a version of you that only they held. When they are gone, that version is also gone. You attend the service and speak to people you recognize and do not quite remember, and the whole afternoon has the quality of the last of something.

One hundred and ninety-six countries agree to limit warming to 1.5 degrees. The agreement is binding in intent and voluntary in mechanism. Scientists say it is insufficient. Diplomats say it is historic. Both are true. The planet will warm past 1.5 degrees. The agreement made it somewhat less warm than it would otherwise have been, which is something, and not enough.

The photograph of the boy on the beach travels around the world in hours. Aylan Kurdi, three years old. More than a million people cross into Europe in 2015, most through the Aegean. The political response divides governments. Hungary builds a fence. Germany says wir schaffen das. The debate happening in newspapers and parliaments is conducted almost entirely without asking what made people leave.
The tests come back quickly. The specialist explains everything clearly. You hav… — The medication stabilises your condition. You adjust to a new normal.
You walk the same route you have walked for years. The route is known to the body. You do not plan the turn. You take it. The walk is its own sufficient thing.
The rosary at the end of the day. The beads are known by touch. The words arrive without thought.

The count runs past midnight. By four in the morning the maps are mostly blue. You look around at the people who voted the same way you did, or didn't, and you understand that this result will take years to mean something — and that whatever it means will be contested. The margin is fifty-two to forty-eight. This turns out to be enough.

The stories begin with Weinstein and don't stop. The naming of something that was understood but not spoken produces a specific discomfort in some rooms and a specific relief in others. You register both, and what you do with them.

A very old woman stops in the middle of the pavement for three or four seconds, with the look of someone who has temporarily lost her place. Then something returns — the head turns, the bearing adjusts, she walks on. You were the only person who noticed. She does not know you saw the three seconds. She has dealt with it and moved on.
You ask people to repeat themselves and they do, and you still only catch most o… — They help substantially. The world comes back. You waited longer than you should have.

You find photographs you had forgotten existed. The person in some of them is you, but a version of you that is distant enough to examine with something approaching objectivity. That person did not know they were at the beginning of things.
The cold takes longer to leave the bones in the morning than it did at fifty.
You have been doing this thing for decades. You do it still.

The news in January is about a virus in one city. By March, you are not allowed to leave your home. The streets are empty. The hospitals are not. By the end of the year, more people have died than in any year since the Second World War. Normal resumes slowly and unevenly, and is not the same normal as before.
The tests come back quickly. The specialist explains everything clearly. You hav… — Your blood sugar stabilises. The condition is managed, if not cured.
El Salvador, 1988. The country is in motion — politically, economically, always. You arrive to a family of modest means but solid foundations.
Hunger is a recurring companion. You understand scarcity before you understand much else.
The tithe is the tithe. The first tenth before anything else.
The Sandinistas, the Contras, and American money. Villages are caught between forces that change meaning depending on who you ask. A generation comes of age in ambiguity and violence.
There is a word you have been practising. You say it when you can.
The rosary at the end of the day. The beads are known by touch. The words arrive without thought.
Your father leaves before it is light. A bag goes with him. Your mother tells yo… — You are told something. The answer does not answer the question. You stop asking, which is its own kind of answer.
The baby has arrived and the house has reorganised itself around it. The baby cr… — You fetch the blanket and hand it carefully to your mother. She says good and that is enough.

You see something in the street from the window — a man being pulled by two other men, or something passing fast — and your mother draws you away from the glass before you can understand what you saw. The not-understanding stays with you longer than seeing would have.
The sound of the adults talking in the next room while you fall asleep.
The fever runs high for four days. There is a discussion among the adults about … — The treatment is unfamiliar and slightly frightening. You recover. Whether it helped is a question nobody settles.

A page that was marks becomes words. You are not sure exactly when it happens — one morning the letters stop being separate things and start being a sentence, and the sentence is about a dog in a field, and the dog is there as clearly as if it were in the room with you. You read it again to make sure.
The early years end. You begin to know where you are.
The boda-boda, okada, tuk-tuk — the driver knows which streets are faster today. You trust this.

At the table nearby, the food is divided with a precision that suggests the calculation has been made many times. The children get more. The parents take less without commenting on it. The children eat without noticing. The parents do not want them to notice.
A bruise appeared on your knee without you remembering how it arrived. This happens frequently.
Auditions for the school play. The drama teacher hands out sheets. You didn't kn… — You don't get it, but you get something — a small part, stage fright, and a curtain call.

There are sixty children in the classroom. One teacher, one board, no textbooks — only the notebook you share with your cousin. The teacher writes on the board and you copy. You have been doing this since you were old enough to hold a pencil. You do not know yet that this is unusual.
The bill arrived. The bill is paid. The paying of it leaves something specific in the account.
There is a teacher who calls on you when you aren't expecting it. Not to embarrass you — to hear you. You can feel the difference immediately.
The friend you sit next to every day for two years. The sitting next to them is the friendship.
The afternoon rain in the rainy season arrives at the same hour every day with the punctuality of a church bell.
The body is changing. The world is starting to require something from you.
You earn a small sum helping at the market or running errands. It is the first m… — The discipline of waiting teaches something permanent.
The pastor's name is known in the neighbourhood. His word carries weight that secular institutions have lost.

In the morning, two planes hit the towers. By afternoon, a third hits the Pentagon and a fourth goes down in a Pennsylvania field. The tallest buildings in New York are gone. You watch the footage repeat on television. What follows — the security lines, the databases, the wars — will outlast everyone alive today.
A group at a party offers you something. Everyone is watching to see what you do… — The night goes somewhere unexpected.

Your body is changing and it is doing so without your permission. People notice before you are ready for them to notice. Some of them say things about it. You learn to carry yourself in a particular way, a new way, that you did not have to think about before.
Overtime was offered. The calculation took a moment. You said yes.
You read it in a book, or hear it spoken on a television programme, or find it i… — Something settles. Not everything, but something. You say the word to yourself in the mirror once. Just to hear it.
You have started to see your teachers as people with lives outside this building. This is not entirely comfortable.
The testimony time — the standing up, the naming of what was given back — is its own form of witnessing.

Someone you trusted tells something you told them in confidence. Not a secret ex… — The confrontation is uncomfortable and real. They apologise in a way that doesn't quite land. The friendship changes permanently.

A summer that feels different from other summers. You are not sure yet why it feels different, or rather you are not sure which of the reasons is the real one. Later you will know that it was the last summer that felt like this kind of summer. At the time you cannot know this. You just notice that something is different.
Broadband has made the internet a permanent fact of the home. It is not turned on and off. It is simply there.
The nearest hospital is hours away or the local clinic is understaffed. The diag… — Rehab is hard in ways you did not anticipate. You come out the other side with tools and a fragile clarity.
The official tells you there is a fee. There is no official fee. You pay, or you wait forever. This is how things work.
You are eighteen. The life begins in earnest.
The body is at the peak it may never discuss. You are not thinking about the peak. You are using it.
You graduate from high school. GPA: 2.52. The world is waiting — what comes next… — A practical path. Two years to a certified trade.

The banks are not actually solid. This becomes clear in September. Firms that existed for a century are gone over a weekend. Governments that said there was no money for schools find money for banks. The unemployment numbers rise for years afterward. The people who caused the crash keep their bonuses.
You are eighteen. The scaffolding of childhood has been removed. The life ahead … — You fix the compass on work and ambition. Other things will have to wait, or happen in the margins.
Trade school — year 1 of 2 (electrician).
The body is what it is right now, which you will not appreciate until later.
Which trade will you train in?… — In-demand work. Good pay.

The city at four in the morning: the people who are awake at four are a subset of the city with its own internal logic. The delivery drivers, the nurses finishing, the insomniacs, the ones who stayed too late at someone else's home. You are briefly among them. Four in the morning makes everyone look like they know something the daytime doesn't.
🔧 You complete your electrician certification.
The week had a middle and an end and the end arrived.

You make a mistake that is clearly yours. There is no version of the story in wh… — The acknowledgement is uncomfortable and brief. Something is repaired, or at least the damage does not spread. You carry the lesson without the weight of the cover-up.

The woman across the landing — you have seen her every morning for two years without learning her name — takes in a package when you're not there. She leaves it inside your door. Nothing is said about it. Nothing needs to be.
The bus stop is the neighbourhood's common room. You know the regulars by face if not by name.
The gang territory: the block that belongs to which side and the route you take that does not cross the wrong territory.

The keys go on the table by the door. This has become automatic. You did not decide that the table by the door would be where the keys go. It assembled itself over weeks of arriving home and putting the keys somewhere. Now the keys go on the table by the door.
The table is set for one. This is a fact and not always just a fact.
The account shows a number that requires decisions to be made immediately. You c… — Someone says yes. The relief is enormous and costs something you cannot quite name. You pay it back.

Walking back one evening, you pass a lit window on the second floor. Inside, someone is sitting at a table — you cannot tell what they are doing, only the quality of their stillness. The whole of their day, their history, their internal weather, is behind that glass. You walk on. The light goes out of view.

The caravans form in San Pedro Sula, in San Salvador, in Guatemala City — families, mostly, moving north. The images are on every screen. The debate that follows them in other countries is conducted almost entirely without asking what made them leave: the gang extortion that functions as a parallel tax on every small business, the poverty that the remittance economy papers over but doesn't fix, the government that cannot protect anyone.
The first apartment: the smell of someone else's cooking in the walls, the window onto the unfamiliar street.

Your country signed an agreement it did not cause to be necessary. The wealthy countries that burned coal and oil for two centuries agreed to slow down, somewhat. Your country — which contributed less than one percent of cumulative emissions — will experience the consequences of what the wealthy countries did. The agreement acknowledges this, in language. The reparations promised in that language remain theoretical.

You are among hundreds of thousands crossing the Mediterranean in an inflatable boat. The crossing takes hours. Some boats sink. Whether you reach land, and which land, and what happens when you do — these are decided by wind and coast guards and the politics of countries that are not yours.

This relationship was not the heartbreak of adolescence — it was a real adult th… — The clarity costs something. In six months you understand why it was right. The understanding does not retroactively reduce the cost.

The library at the end of the night, when most people have left. One or two others remain — at separate tables, in separate silences. You do not know what they are working on. They do not know what you are working on. The shared silence is its own form of companionship.
The nearest hospital is hours away or the local clinic is understaffed. The diag… — The medication quiets the alarm system enough to live with. Some days are genuinely fine.
The market is your domain — the price-checking, the weight, the recognition of freshness. The vendor knows you.
You are thirty. The life you have been building has become recognizable as a life.

The account balance is the kind of number that means decisions have been made for you. You count the days until the next payment. Each day is a small calculation. You walk past things in shops without stopping — not because you are disciplined, but because stopping would cost something you do not have.
The smell of something cooking is the first announcement that someone is home.
You are thirty. The life you have been building has become recognizable as a lif… — The focus narrows. What exists gets deeper rather than wider.
A person who used to be young is now the age you were when you thought of them as young. This happens continuously.

The news in January is about a virus in one city. By March, you are not allowed to leave your home. The streets are empty. The hospitals are not. By the end of the year, more people have died than in any year since the Second World War. Normal resumes slowly and unevenly, and is not the same normal as before.
The nearest hospital is hours away or the local clinic is understaffed. The diag… — The medication lifts the fog enough to function. It is not happiness, but it is solid ground.
Uzbek SSR, 1952. The maternity ward smells of disinfectant. Your mother was not allowed to have your father in the room.
Your early years are warm. There is food, laughter, and a sense that the world is safe.

Your grandparent keeps you while your parents work. The day has a different texture — slower, full of a kind of attention you don't get elsewhere. There are stories about before you existed. You don't understand all of them. You keep them anyway.
Overtime was offered. The calculation took a moment. You said yes.
The seasons are the years. The year is measured from planting to harvest to the dry to the rains.
South Africa, 1978. The city is enormous and still growing. The neighbourhood you are born into will shape everything that follows.
Your parent disappears into something chemical. The person who returns from it is not quite the same person who left.
The road to the town: the distance is known in time, not in kilometres. The time depends on the season.
Race determines where you live, where you work, what school you attend. The law enforces a hierarchy that crushes some and privileges others.
There is a word you have been practising. You say it when you can.
The bill arrived. The bill is paid. The paying of it leaves something specific in the account.
You take your first wobbly steps across the living room floor. Your parents beam… — You make it three steps before sitting down hard. Everyone cheers.

You do not know the word for your situation yet. You know that some children at the edge of your world have things you don't. You know that the adults manage their faces when certain subjects come up. You are storing this without being able to say what it is.
The baby has arrived and the house has reorganised itself around it. The baby cr… — You fetch the blanket and hand it carefully to your mother. She says good and that is enough.
The returnee: someone who went to the city and came back. The reasons for coming back are not always freely given.

The lights go out in the evening, and the next morning too. Your parent is on the phone for a long time using a voice you know means something is wrong. The electricity company has cut the service. Your neighbour lets you charge a torch from their socket. For two weeks you do your homework by the light of it.
The body that knows the season before the calendar does: the knee, the shoulder, what the rain will do.
The early years end. You begin to know where you are.
The same dream keeps coming back. Something is in the dark at the end of the hal… — They sit with you until the room feels safe again. It helps more than you can articulate.
Your mother says something small and it lands the wrong way — not cruelty, just carelessness. You carry it out of the room and down the street and further than that.
You have memorised the route between school and home the way the body memorises routes — without effort, just presence.
You are memorising things without meaning to: routes, faces, sequences, the way the light falls.

Your grandparent has a silence about one decade that they do not have about other decades. You notice it without understanding it. When you ask about those years, they say something general that covers the years without opening them. The general answer is technically true. The silence around it is more informative.
You did the ordinary thing on the ordinary day and it added up to the year.
You want a pet more than you've wanted anything in your short life. You dream ab… — Your parents say no firmly and repeatedly until you drop it.

In Rural Eastern Cape everyone knows what you did last Tuesday. The privacy you will later want does not exist here, and neither does the loneliness. The village watches you grow up the way it watches the seasons, with matter-of-fact attention. This is its own kind of being held.
You looked out the window at a particular moment. What you saw was unremarkable. You looked at it anyway.

You go to the city for the first time. The noise. The smell of exhaust and food and people in numbers you have never seen. The buildings are larger than you pictured. You lose your companion in a crowd for twenty minutes and the feeling of it — pure, vertiginous panic — is also a kind of education.
The body is changing. The world is starting to require something from you — and you, more than most, feel the pressure to answer.
The city has a formal face and an informal one. You grew up in the informal one … —
You think in one language and write in another. At home you are one person. At school you are a translation of that person. The gap between them is where you live now.
The dust: in the lungs, in the house, in the ten-year health projection everyone in this town shares.
Sleep has reorganised — later to bed, harder to wake — and no one understands this is not entirely a choice.
The group that controls the block is not officially anything. They collect somet… — Invisible is a skill. You develop it.
The wage is what it is. You know what day it arrives and what needs to come out of it first.

The first free election. The queues go for miles. People who have never voted in their country stand in them for hours without complaint. The vote cast is the weight of everything that came before it. The transition is not the end of what apartheid did — the land, the wealth, the schools remain organized by the old logic — but it is something new and real happening in a place that needed it.
Mandela is president. The world is watching. Inside the country, the texture of daily life is changing in ways both dramatic and glacially slow. History is happening and so is Tuesday.
Something happens — a test result, a comparison, a public moment — that makes th… — You do not stop. The wound is there but you will not let it stop you. This becomes a pattern.
A stranger does something for you that they had no reason to do. It isn't large. You remember it for years.
The school corridor between lessons: the economy of the seven minutes — who is speaking to whom, who is not.

The internet moves from universities to homes, then to pockets. What information means, what distance means, what a shop is, what a friend is — all of it is changing faster than anyone can name accurately. The rules are being made up as it goes.
Someone posts something unflattering about you online. It's spreading fast.… — The drama escalates briefly, then dies down. People have short memories.
Someone else received credit for the kind of work you also do. You are still deciding what the correct feeling is.
You are eighteen. The proof-of-worth project has a new arena now. The life begins in earnest.
A day when the rain makes the decision for you. You stay inside. There is a particular freedom in having the choice removed.
You graduate from high school. GPA: 2.54. The world is waiting — what comes next… — A practical path. Two years to a certified trade.
You are eighteen. The scaffolding of childhood has been removed. The life ahead … — You fix the compass on work and ambition. Other things will have to wait, or happen in the margins.
Trade school — year 1 of 2 (electrician).
The walk to the market, the school, the well. The route is known. The feet know it.
Which trade will you train in?… — In-demand work. Good pay.

In the station, everyone is going somewhere and has reasons for it. The man with the too-heavy bag has reasons. The woman in the yellow coat has reasons. The child running ahead of the parent has only speed as a reason, which is also a kind of reason. You find your platform. Everyone else finds theirs. The station empties in all directions at once.
🔧 You complete your electrician certification.
The clinic is busy. You wait two hours. The doctor is straightforward. Treatment… — The medication lifts the fog enough to function. It is not happiness, but it is solid ground.

The keys go on the table by the door. This has become automatic. You did not decide that the table by the door would be where the keys go. It assembled itself over weeks of arriving home and putting the keys somewhere. Now the keys go on the table by the door.
The text message arrives. Its abbreviations are now fluent to you.

In the morning, two planes hit the towers. By afternoon, a third hits the Pentagon and a fourth goes down in a Pennsylvania field. The tallest buildings in New York are gone. You watch the footage repeat on television. What follows — the security lines, the databases, the wars — will outlast everyone alive today.

You are the only person who looks like you in the room. This happens often enough that you have a routine for it: arrive early, be confident, do not mention it, navigate the particular kind of invisibility and hyper-visibility that happen simultaneously.
The weekend is real — two days in which the body does something other than what it is told.

A small child waves at you from a window. You are a stranger to them — you are waving at someone who has not yet learned to need a reason to wave at a stranger. You wave back. The child seems satisfied. The transaction is complete.

At the market, a vendor wraps your purchase with the efficiency of someone who has done this ten thousand times. The motion of their hands is exact and unhurried. Their whole working day is in the motion. You take the package. They look past you at the next person in line. The transaction is complete.
You went somewhere and came back and the somewhere and the coming back were both ordinary.
You need income. The options in front of you are not glamorous.… — The work is humbling. The paycheck is real. You are employed.
You begin working as a Day Laborer. Starting salary: $1,945/yr.
A confrontation at a bar escalates faster than expected. Someone throws a punch.… — You swing.
Bruised ribs, split lip. An expensive lesson.

Walking back one evening, you pass a lit window on the second floor. Inside, someone is sitting at a table — you cannot tell what they are doing, only the quality of their stillness. The whole of their day, their history, their internal weather, is behind that glass. You walk on. The light goes out of view.
You are promoted to Skilled Laborer. New salary: $5,200/yr.
The office and the accent and the clothes and the calibration of how much to explain about where you started.
The bus fare is counted before the bus is caught.

The banks are not actually solid. This becomes clear in September. Firms that existed for a century are gone over a weekend. Governments that said there was no money for schools find money for banks. The unemployment numbers rise for years afterward. The people who caused the crash keep their bonuses.
You are thirty. The proving has been ongoing. You are beginning to notice whether it is working.
You have built something demonstrable by now — a career, a record, a reputation … — You commit. The other path closes, as paths do when you stop looking at them.

You remembered a dream into the afternoon, which is unusual. Most dreams leave by nine. This one stayed. You are not sure what it means and are not inclined to interpret it formally, but it occupied a specific corner of the day.
You are promoted to Foreman. New salary: $8,241/yr.
The grey in the hair has reached a percentage people comment on. You have decided how to respond.
You are thirty. The scoreboard you have been keeping — achievements, titles, the… — The focus narrows. What exists gets deeper rather than wider.
There was a meeting where you knew something. You didn't say it. Afterward you sat with the old familiar feeling — having chosen correctly and still losing something.
A person who used to be young is now the age you were when you thought of them as young. This happens continuously.

In the language you grew up with, the vocabulary for this is not exactly absent — but it is not for speaking aloud. What happens between people is private in a specific way: not like a secret, but like weather. Everyone knows it is there. No one describes it directly. You have lived your whole life in this silence and it does not feel like deprivation. It is simply how the thing is held.
The diaspora family is in the group chat. The group chat is the compound, extended.

There is a period — it lasts perhaps two or three years — when you are at the exact intersection of experience and energy. You know what you are doing and you still have the stamina to do it well. The problems your work presents are the right size. You think about work on the train home, not with dread but with something closer to absorption. You will not call it happiness, exactly. It is more like: fit.
There is something you are putting off. You know this. You are still putting it off.
You have started saying things your parents said. The things are not wrong. They are just finally true.

Your country signed an agreement it did not cause to be necessary. The wealthy countries that burned coal and oil for two centuries agreed to slow down, somewhat. Your country — which contributed less than one percent of cumulative emissions — will experience the consequences of what the wealthy countries did. The agreement acknowledges this, in language. The reparations promised in that language remain theoretical.
A contractor did substandard work that cost you significantly. Your solicitor sa… — The case takes eighteen months. You receive a settlement that partially covers your losses.
The smartphone is the last thing checked at night and the first thing checked in the morning. You have noticed this.
Your brother calls. Your mother is gone. She was fine two weeks ago. He does not… — The drive is long. You arrive to a house that already feels different — the same objects, the wrong order.

You woke at three in the morning with a thought that did not warrant waking at three in the morning. The thought knows no proportionality. You lay with it for an hour. It dissolved into sleep before it resolved.

The stories begin with Weinstein and don't stop. The naming of something that was understood but not spoken produces a specific discomfort in some rooms and a specific relief in others. You register both, and what you do with them.
A junior colleague is struggling with a presentation — work you both contributed… — They present it well. The room applauds. You clap. Nobody names what this cost you, because nobody knows.

The proof has accumulated. You have what you worked for. The question that surfaces at forty, quietly, is whether the accumulation has addressed the original doubt. You have a feeling you already know the answer.
The specific sound of your home at night. The pipes. The floorboard. The thing the street does at a certain hour.
The clinic is busy. You wait two hours. The doctor is straightforward. Treatment… — You complete the programme but relapse within the year. Recovery is rarely linear.
Azerbaijan SSR, 1928. The maternity ward smells of disinfectant. Your mother was not allowed to have your father in the room.

Born in a Soviet maternity ward, where your mother was given no pain relief and told not to make noise. Your father waited outside for three days. You were wrapped in hospital standard-issue cloth and handed over at the gate.
The specific sound of your home at night. The pipes. The floorboard. The thing the street does at a certain hour.
Your early years are warm. There is food, laughter, and a sense that the world is safe.
A small thing happened that you will not remember in ten years. At the time it occupied you for an afternoon.
The year has a rhythm — certain smells, certain light, certain changes in what the adults do. You are storing this rhythm without knowing you are storing it.
The year passed in the way that years pass — faster than the days suggested it would.

Your grandparent keeps you while your parents work. The day has a different texture — slower, full of a kind of attention you don't get elsewhere. There are stories about before you existed. You don't understand all of them. You keep them anyway.
No electricity means the evening is lit by fire or lamp. The evening has a size that electricity did not give it.

The global economy has contracted sharply. Prices for what your country sells abroad have fallen; prices for what your country buys have not. The adjustment is paid for by people who had no part in creating the crisis.
The early years end. You begin to know where you are.
There is one thing that belongs to you. A piece of bright cloth, or a tin car wi… — You find a place for it that only you know. It stays yours.
Eid — the prayer, the clothes, the greeting, the food, the visiting. The year has anchors and Eid is one of them.
Every evening after regular school, you go to the mosque or the madrassa to lear… — By age ten you can recite three juz from memory. Your parents are proud. The teacher gives you a new name: Hafiz.
Someone makes clear, without saying it, that you are a category before you are a person. You file this away. You are not sure yet what to do with it.

Your family is moving. New house, new neighborhood, new school in September. You… — For a while, you do. Then the calls get shorter. Then they stop. Then one resurfaces on social media fifteen years later.
You are memorising things without meaning to: routes, faces, sequences, the way the light falls.

A group you considered yourself part of does not include you in something. You find out later — through the carelessness of someone who assumed you already knew, or through the specific silence of people who share a thing you are not sharing. You say nothing about it to anyone. You reassess.
The letter took two weeks to arrive and another two weeks to receive a response. The slow conversation has its own rhythm.
A group at school has made you their target. Every day is an exercise in dread m… — The intervention is imperfect. It helps. The bullying slows.

You see an adult crying — not at a funeral, not in the context that makes it make sense to you. Just at a table, or on the step outside, in the middle of an ordinary day. Adults cry for reasons you do not yet have. The world is larger than the reasons you know the names of.
A grandparent died this year. The house had more people in it, and then fewer, and then a different quiet.
The body is changing. The constraints that felt manageable in childhood feel unbearable now.
Fajr: the dawn prayer before the light. The body knows this hour now.
The university entrance exam is approaching. Everything seems to depend on it.… — The score opens doors you did not know existed.

Someone at school has a cassette tape. Western music — American rock, British pop. It is technically contraband. You listen to it in someone's bedroom with the volume down, and for 45 minutes the world seems larger than it is.
The apartment building's staircase is lit or it is not. You know which it is.
A scholarship application lands in front of you. It would pay for university.… — You get it. The letter arrives on a Tuesday.
You have started to see your teachers as people with lives outside this building. This is not entirely comfortable.
A limit arrives — from family, school, circumstance — and the familiar tightenin… — You do not stop. The wound is there but you will not let it stop you. This becomes a pattern.
You get a small job — something after school or on weekends. The first paycheck … — At the end of the month the number is still there. This is not nothing.
The adhan sounds from the minaret or the phone or the radio, and the day organises itself around it.
You are eighteen. The life you were handed is behind you. The one you choose begins now.
Everyone is doing something and whether you join or don't is the thing being wat… — You belong, temporarily. The price of belonging is what you did or didn't do and whether you can live with that.
The war is elsewhere and also here: in the prices, in what is not available, in the letters.
You graduate from high school. GPA: 2.86. The world is waiting — what comes next… — You earn a partial scholarship and enroll in university.
The freedom is exactly that: the ability to be somewhere else by Thursday if it seems right.
You are eighteen. The scaffolding of childhood has been removed. The life ahead … — You fix the compass on work and ambition. Other things will have to wait, or happen in the margins.

A man comes out of the maternity ward entrance and stands on the pavement. He is not making a phone call yet. He is just standing. His face has the quality of someone who has just witnessed something enormous and is not yet ready to speak it into the smaller world outside. Then he takes out his phone.
University — year 1 of 4 (healthcare).
You cooked a meal for yourself. The silence after is the kitchen at night.
What will you study at university?… — Demanding, long hours, significant reward.
University — year 2 of 4 (healthcare).
You have watched three people from the village leave. You know what they have now and what they no longer have.
The official tells you there is a fee. There is no official fee. You pay, or you wait forever. This is how things work.
University — year 3 of 4 (healthcare).
You have an idea. It might be a business. It might be a mistake.… — The first year nearly breaks you.

Walking back one evening, you pass a lit window on the second floor. Inside, someone is sitting at a table — you cannot tell what they are doing, only the quality of their stillness. The whole of their day, their history, their internal weather, is behind that glass. You walk on. The light goes out of view.
🎓 You graduate from university with a degree in healthcare. The first in your family to do so. Final GPA: 2.98.
Everything necessary is within walking distance or requires the acceptance of a long walk.
This is not what you thought work would be. The gap between what you imagined an… — You become efficient at it without meaning to. Efficiency is not the same as caring. You know the difference.
The view from the window at the same angle for years. The view has changed slightly. The angle has not.
El Salvador, 1963. The country is in motion — politically, economically, always. You arrive to a family of modest means but solid foundations.
